title: Make put_tree atomic — wrap in explicit transaction
description:
put_tree inserts into merkle_cache, merkle_tree_nodes, and merkle_leaf_info as separate auto-committed statements. If the merkle_leaf_info INSERT fails (e.g., UNIQUE constraint violation from a concurrent handler), the earlier two inserts are already committed, leaving the cache in an Incomplete state (CacheStatus::Incomplete) forever.

This is directly causing 37% of files (9764/26201) to be reclassified as Incomplete on every startup. The background check's recompute_file deletes the stale entry, then both the background check and a concurrent handler try to put_tree. The handler wins, then the background check's put_tree hits the UNIQUE constraint on merkle_leaf_info(file_path, chunk_hash). The first two tables succeed, the third fails → Incomplete → next startup reclassifies as Incomplete → infinite recompute loop.

Fix: wrap the three INSERT statements in put_tree inside an explicit BEGIN IMMEDIATE / COMMIT transaction so a failure on any insert rolls back all three. Also use INSERT OR REPLACE on merkle_leaf_info (or DELETE + INSERT within the same transaction) to handle the case where a concurrent handler already inserted those rows.

## Context
Observed in production: 9764/26201 files incomplete on every startup. Logs show UNIQUE constraint failed: merkle_leaf_info.file_path, merkle_leaf_info.chunk_hash during background check.
acceptance_criteria: put_tree is fully atomic: if merkle_leaf_info INSERT fails, merkle_cache and merkle_tree_nodes inserts are rolled back. No Incomplete state left behind after a put_tree failure. Test: concurrent put_tree calls for the same file_path succeed (last-write-wins) without UNIQUE constraint errors leaving partial state.

title: Fix verify_node inconsistency for single-child Merkle nodes
description:
BUG: MerkleTree::verify_node() treats single-child intermediate nodes as identity (parent == child), but build_with_cache() hashes them (parent = BLAKE3(child_bytes)). This makes files with certain chunk counts unreadable via FUSE.

The bug triggers when an intermediate Merkle tree level has a single-child group. This happens when ceil(n/64^k) mod 64 == 1 for some level k. Examples: 65 chunks, 4097 chunks, 262145 chunks.

For most file sizes (including 1GB with ~8000 chunks), the bug does NOT trigger because intermediate levels don't produce single-child groups at those sizes. But it IS a correctness bug that makes specific file sizes unreadable.

The inconsistency in crates/rift-common/src/crypto.rs:
- build/build_with_cache (line ~237, ~343): always hashes children, even single-child groups → parent = BLAKE3(child_bytes)
- verify_node (line ~263): returns parent == children[0] for single-child groups → expects identity
- The docstring says 'same hashing scheme as build()' but it differs

Client code in view.rs:165 calls verify_node on drill responses. When a single-child intermediate node is drilled into, verify_node fails.

Fix (recommended): Change verify_node to hash single-child groups like build_with_cache does. Remove the special case. Update the docstring.

title: Server: fix read path to call put_tree instead of put_merkle
description:
Bug: the cold READ path in handler/read.rs calls update_merkle_cache() which calls db.put_merkle(). This only populates 1 of 3 database tables (merkle_cache). It does NOT populate merkle_tree_nodes or merkle_leaf_info.

As a result, after a cold READ:
- get_merkle() works (returns the MerkleEntry with root hash + leaf hashes)
- get_all_leaf_info() returns None (merkle_leaf_info table is empty for this file)
- The warm path in stream_cached_read() calls get_all_leaf_info() to get chunk offsets/lengths, gets None, falls back to cold path

This means even with a real Database cache, warm-path READ doesn't work after a cold READ — it always falls back. The drill handler (drill.rs) correctly calls put_tree(), but the read handler doesn't.

Fix: Change the cold READ path to compute and store the full Merkle tree (including leaf_info), not just the root and leaf hashes. This means:
1. In handler/read.rs, after computing leaf_hashes in the cold path, also compute and store leaf_info (offset, length, chunk_index for each leaf)
2. Call db.put_tree() instead of db.put_merkle() — or add leaf info to the existing put_merkle call
3. Alternative: add a new method or adapt update_merkle_cache to optionally store full tree data

Key file references:
- handler/read.rs:231-235 — update_merkle_cache calls put_merkle
- handler/drill.rs:89-107 — build_and_cache_tree correctly calls put_tree
- metadata/merkle.rs:141-219 — put_tree() stores all 3 tables atomically
- metadata/merkle.rs:82-131 — put_merkle() only stores merkle_cache table
- handler/merkle_cache_trait.rs — MerkleCache trait definition

Tests: verify that after a cold READ, a subsequent warm READ hits the cache (no re-chunking). Verify get_all_leaf_info returns data after put_tree is called.

title: Server: wire up Database instead of NoopCache in main.rs
description:
The server uses NoopCache (a no-op stub) as its MerkleCache implementation in main.rs:102. This makes the warm path completely dead code — every get_merkle() returns None, every put_merkle() silently discards data. The real Database (SQLite-backed, persistent, with mtime staleness checks) exists in metadata/db.rs but is never instantiated.

Fix: Replace the NoopCache type with Database in main.rs. Add a db_path config option (default: platform data dir). The RequestContext and all handlers are already generic over MerkleCache, so no handler code needs to change.

Current code (main.rs:102):
  let db: Arc<rift_server::handler::NoopCache> = Arc::new(rift_server::handler::NoopCache);

Should become:
  let db_path = server_config.db_path.clone().unwrap_or_else(|| ...);
  let db: Arc<rift_server::metadata::db::Database> = Arc::new(Database::open(&db_path).await?);

The Database type already implements MerkleCache (see merkle_cache_trait.rs:52-94), including:
- get_merkle() with automatic mtime/size staleness checks
- put_merkle(), put_tree() for writing
- get_all_leaf_info() for warm-path chunk info

Also add ServerConfig.db_path field (Option<PathBuf>) and a CLI flag --db-path for specifying the SQLite database location. Default to dirs::data_local_dir()/rift/merkle_cache.db.

Tests: verify that a Database-backed server correctly caches Merkle entries across requests (first request cold, second warm). Verify stale entries are evicted when files change.

notes: Also fixed cache reconstruction to verify per-chunk hashes — prevents serving stale corrupted data when root hash matches but individual chunks are wrong. Added remove_manifest() for evicting corrupted cache entries.

This eliminated the ability to use InMemoryListener for fast unit tests. Two server unit tests (accept_loop_accepts_and_handles_a_connection, accept_loop_exits_when_listener_closes) were deleted as a result. title: SECURITY: merkle_drill handler bypasses resolve() path traversal guard
description: merkle_drill_response() in handler.rs uses handle_db.get_path(&handle) directly (line 690) instead of calling resolve(). Every other handler uses resolve(), which: (1) canonicalizes the path (resolves symlinks), (2) verifies the canonical path doesn't escape the share root. By skipping resolve(), the drill handler could serve Merkle tree data for paths outside the share via symlink race or handle injection. Fix: replace handle_db.get_path(&handle) with resolve(share, &handle, handle_db).await. Add a test that registers a handle pointing outside the share and verifies the drill handler rejects it.

title: Refactor: canonicalize_within_share symlink handling
description:
SECURITY: Fix TOCTOU symlink bypass in canonicalize_within_share

## Problem
In security.rs::fd_recanonicalize_linux, when symlink_metadata detects a symlink, the function returns Some(canonical) without fd-based verification. This allows a TOCTOU race where a file inside share can be swapped for a symlink pointing outside share.

## Root Cause
Lines ~100-105 in security.rs:
```rust
if meta.is_symlink() || meta.is_dir() {
    return Some(canonical.to_path_buf());  // BYPASSES fd verification!
}
```

If a file is replaced with a symlink between canonicalize() and this check, the symlink target escapes containment.

## Fix
Change symlink handling to log warning and return None (skip the file):

```rust
if meta.is_symlink() {
    tracing::warn!(
        path = %canonical.display(),
        "TOCTOU race: file became symlink during canonicalization, skipping"
    );
    return None;
}
```

Note: Directories still return Some(canonical) since fd verification isn't applicable.

## Files
- crates/rift-server/src/security.rs

## Tests Required
- Red: Write test that creates file, swaps to symlink during canonicalize window, verify None returned
- Green: Implement fix
- Verify existing tests still pass

title: Option C walk: send only paths, stat at process time, transactional upsert
description:
walk_share stats each file on the blocking thread, then sends FileInfo { path, mtime_ns, file_size } through the MPSC channel. By the time handle_file processes it, the file may have changed — stale mtime/size causes false CacheStatus::Stale, triggering unnecessary recomputation. Worse, recompute_file calls delete_merkle then cache_computed_tree — a TOCTOU window where a handler writes the cache between delete and recompute.

Fix (Option C):
1. Change channel to send only PathBuf (remove FileInfo struct)
2. walk_share sends PathBuf only
3. handle_file calls std::fs::metadata before cache_status
4. recompute_file becomes a single put_tree call (no separate delete_merkle)
5. Add TOCTOU test: modify file between walk and process, verify no stale/mismatched cache entry
close_reason: Implemented Option C walk in branch option-c-walk (commit 778caed)

title: Share-containment canonicalization helper
description:
background_check.rs::walk_share calls canonicalize() with NO containment check — a symlink race could escape the share boundary. handler/mod.rs already has is_within_share() + fd_recanonicalize_linux() for this exact purpose.

Extract a shared helper:
  pub fn canonicalize_within_share(path: &Path, share_root: &Path) -> Option<PathBuf>

- Reuse is_within_share logic + fd_recanonicalize_linux for Linux
- Apply in walk_share for each directory entry before descending
- Apply in recompute_file before passing path to cache_computed_tree
- Remove duplicated containment logic from handler/mod.rs
close_reason: Implemented canonicalize_within_share helper in branch bg-check-containment (commit ec1aa5b)

title: Safe canonicalize helper for share containment
description:
Every piece of code that resolves a filesystem path (6+ sites in handler/, 1 in background_check, 1 in handle.rs) independently calls canonicalize() and then — sometimes — checks containment via is_within_share(). The background check does canonicalize() with NO containment check, meaning symlinks/hardlinks pointing outside the share root are followed without restriction. The handlers have a more rigorous but duplicated pattern (canonicalize → containment check → fd re-canonicalize on Linux).

Create a safe_canonicalize(path, share_root) -> Result<PathBuf> function in src/safe_path.rs that:
- Resolves symlinks via canonicalize()
- Verifies the result starts with the canonicalized share root
- Returns an error if the path escapes the share
- On Linux, provides a safe_canonicalize_fd() variant that re-opens by canonical path and re-canonicalizes via /proc/self/fd/N (matching existing fd_recanonicalize_linux pattern)

Replace all canonicalize() call sites with the new helper:
- handler/mod.rs: resolve_path() and symlink resolution
- handler/read.rs: 7 call sites
- handler/drill.rs: 2 call sites
- background_check.rs: walk_share()
- handle.rs: xattr handling

Remove is_within_share() as standalone, fold into helper. Remove canonicalize_share_root(), fold into helper or keep as one-time call that feeds share_root param.

## Context
Code review finding #2 from bg-check PR. Background_check.rs walk_share has no share containment check after canonicalize().
acceptance_criteria: All canonicalize() calls outside safe_path.rs use the new helper. walk_share skips files resolving outside share with tracing::debug!. Existing handler behavior preserved. New unit tests: path escapes share → error, path within share → Ok, symlink outside → error.

title: Fix manifest storing all leaves but only some chunks on disk
Cold-path performance: eliminate double-read regression
description:
The streaming cold-path (rift-b0er.1.3) reads the file twice: once for chunk_stream() boundary detection, then again for per-chunk seek+read+hash. The old tokio::fs::read() was a single bulk read — our optimization is actually slower (~2× I/O on HDD).

Fix: Use AsyncStreamCDC::read_chunk() (or as_stream()) which yields ChunkData { offset, length, data } containing both boundaries AND data in a single pass. Hash each chunk as it's produced. Never re-open or re-read the file.

The fastcdc crate (v3.2.1, tokio feature already enabled) provides AsyncStreamCDC with read_chunk() that returns data alongside boundaries. Modify the cold path to:
1. Open file once, create AsyncStreamCDC with BufReader
2. Call read_chunk() in a loop — each call returns boundary + chunk data
3. Hash each chunk immediately via Blake3Hash::new(&chunk.data)
4. Accumulate leaf hashes for Merkle root
5. Send matching chunks to client

Also fix the same double-read pattern in:
- handler/merkle_cache.rs:get_or_compute_merkle_root
- handler/drill.rs:build_and_cache_tree

Estimated impact: ~2× I/O reduction on cold path, restores performance parity with pre-streaming code.

title: Client: refactor read_chunks to process chunks incrementally
Client: fix assemble_byte_range to avoid double-buffering
Client: change ChunkData.data from Vec<u8> to Bytes
description:
# Client: Change ChunkData.data from Vec<u8> to Bytes

## What

Change `ChunkData.data` from `Vec<u8>` to `Bytes`, removing the `.to_vec()` copy per chunk in `read_chunks()`.

## Why

After the codec returns `Bytes` (previous task), the data path is:
```
recv_frame() → Bytes → .to_vec() → Vec<u8> (in ChunkData.data)
```

The `.to_vec()` is now the only remaining unnecessary copy. By making `ChunkData.data` a `Bytes`, we eliminate it entirely:
```
recv_frame() → Bytes → Bytes (in ChunkData.data, same allocation, refcount increment)
```

## How

### 1. Change `ChunkData` struct in `client/client.rs`:
```rust
// Before:
pub struct ChunkData {
    pub index: u32,
    pub length: u64,
    pub hash: [u8; 32],
    pub data: Vec<u8>,
}

// After:
pub struct ChunkData {
    pub index: u32,
    pub length: u64,
    pub hash: [u8; 32],
    pub data: Bytes,
}
```

### 2. Remove `.to_vec()` in `read_chunks()`:
```rust
// Before:
data: data_payload.to_vec(),

// After:
data: data_payload,  // Bytes, no copy
```

### 3. Update callers that currently expect `Vec<u8>`:
- `verify_chunks_integrity` in `view.rs` — `&chunk.data` works with `Bytes` (implements `AsRef<[u8]>`)
- `assemble_byte_range` in `view.rs` — `chunk.data[slice_start..slice_end]` works with `Bytes` (implements `Index`)
- FUSE layer `read()` — `Bytes::from(data)` becomes just `data` (already Bytes)
- Client tests that assert on `ChunkData.data`

### 4. Update `ShareView::read()` return type consideration:
`ShareView::read()` currently returns `Vec<u8>`. For now, keep it as `Vec<u8>` but note that assembling from `Bytes` chunks into `Vec<u8>` is a single allocation (only the needed range), not the double-buffering of today.

## TDD Approach

1. **Write failing test**: Test that `ChunkData.data` is `Bytes` type
2. Change the struct field and remove `.to_vec()`
3. Fix compilation errors in `view.rs`, `fuse.rs`, and test files
4. Verify all tests pass

## Files Changed

- `crates/rift-client/src/client.rs` — `ChunkData` struct + `read_chunks`
- `crates/rift-client/src/view.rs` — `verify_chunks_integrity`, `assemble_byte_range`
- `crates/rift-client/src/fuse.rs` — `read()` handler (simplifies)
- `crates/rift-client/src/client.rs` (tests)
- `crates/rift-client/src/view.rs` (tests)

## Dependencies

- Requires `decode_message` returning `Bytes` (rift-b0er.2.1)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `ChunkData.data` is `Bytes` instead of `Vec<u8>`
- [ ] No `.to_vec()` copy per chunk in `read_chunks`
- [ ] All client tests pass
- [ ] FUSE layer simplified (data is already `Bytes`)

git worktree add ../rift-bytes phase1-bytes  # same branch as codec change

title: Client codec: change decode_message to return Bytes (zero-copy)
description:
# Client Codec: Change decode_message to Return Bytes (Zero-Copy)

## What

Change `decode_message()` in `crates/rift-protocol/src/codec.rs` to return `Bytes` instead of `Vec<u8>`, eliminating one allocation + one copy per frame (especially impactful for BLOCK_DATA frames carrying 128-512 KB chunk payloads).

## Why

Current code in `codec.rs`:
```rust
let payload = buf.split_to(length).to_vec();  // ALLOCATES Vec + COPIES
Ok(Some((type_id as u8, payload)))
```

Then in `quic.rs` `recv_frame()`:
```rust
return Ok(Some((type_id, Bytes::from(payload))));  // wraps Vec into Bytes (no copy, but Vec already allocated)
```

Then in `client.rs` `read_chunks()`:
```rust
data: data_payload.to_vec(),  // COPIES Bytes → Vec<u8> (another copy!)
```

Data flow: `BytesMut → Vec<u8> (copy) → Bytes (wrap) → Vec<u8> (copy again)`.

With `Bytes` return type:
```rust
let payload = buf.split_to(length).freeze();  // BytesMut → Bytes, ZERO-COPY (just refcount increment)
Ok(Some((type_id as u8, payload)))
```

Data flow: `BytesMut → Bytes (zero-copy) → used directly (zero-copy)`.

## How

### 1. Change signature in `codec.rs`:
```rust
// Before:
pub fn decode_message(buf: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<Option<(u8, Vec<u8>)>, CodecError>

// After:
pub fn decode_message(buf: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<Option<(u8, Bytes)>, CodecError>
```

Change implementation:
```rust
let payload = buf.split_to(length).freeze();  // .freeze() instead of .to_vec()
```

### 2. Update `RiftStream::recv_frame` in `transport/quic.rs`:
```rust
// Before:
return Ok(Some((type_id, Bytes::from(payload))));

// After:
return Ok(Some((type_id, payload)));  // payload is already Bytes
```

### 3. Update `InMemoryStream::recv_frame` in `transport/connection.rs`:
Already returns `(u8, Bytes)` — no change needed if codec test double also returns Bytes.

### 4. Update all codec tests:
Change assertions from `Vec<u8>` to `Bytes` comparison. `Bytes` implements `PartialEq<[u8]>` so most asserts can stay as-is.

### 5. Don't change `ChunkData.data` yet (that's a separate task).

## TDD Approach

1. **Write failing test**: Add a new test in `codec.rs` that asserts `decode_message`'s return type is `Result<Option<(u8, Bytes)>, _>`
2. Change the return type and `.freeze()` call
3. Fix all existing tests to compile with the new return type
4. Verify all tests pass

## Files Changed

- `crates/rift-protocol/src/codec.rs` — return type change + `.freeze()`
- `crates/rift-transport/src/quic.rs` — remove `Bytes::from()` wrapper in `recv_frame`
- `crates/rift-protocol/src/codec.rs` (tests) — update assertions
- `crates/rift-transport/src/connection.rs` (tests) — may need minor updates

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `decode_message` returns `Bytes` instead of `Vec<u8>`
- [ ] `.freeze()` used instead of `.to_vec()` (zero-copy)
- [ ] `QuicStream::recv_frame` no longer wraps in `Bytes::from()`
- [ ] All codec tests pass
- [ ] All transport tests pass
- [ ] Wire format is unchanged

## Worktree

```bash
git worktree add ../rift-bytes phase1-bytes
```

title: Server read: warm-path streaming from cached chunk boundaries
description:
# Server Read: Warm-Path Streaming from Cached Chunk Boundaries

## What

When the Merkle cache has chunk boundaries (warm cache), use them to read individual chunks from the file via `tokio::fs::File` and stream them incrementally — no full-file read needed.

## Why

Currently `handler/read.rs:read_response` calls `tokio::fs::read(&canonical)` to load the entire file into a `Vec<u8>` before chunking and streaming. When the Merkle cache is warm, the server already knows chunk boundaries from prior stat/read operations. It can open the file, seek to each chunk's offset, read just that chunk, and send it immediately.

Peak memory: `O(file_size)` → `O(max_chunk_size)` ≈ 512 KB.

## How

### Current flow:
```
tokio::fs::read(path) → Vec<u8> (entire file) → chunker.chunk() → stream_block_frames()
```

### Proposed warm-path flow:
```
db.get_merkle() → Some(cached_entry) → 
  for each chunk in range:
    file.seek(offset) → file.read_exact(length) → 512KB buf →
    verify hash → send BLOCK_HEADER → send BLOCK_DATA
  build Merkle root from cached leaf hashes → send TRANSFER_COMPLETE
```

### Changes in `handler/read.rs`:

1. **Before** the `tokio::fs::read` call, check the Merkle cache:
```rust
// Try warm path first
if let Ok(Some(cached)) = db.get_merkle(&canonical).await {
    // Use cached chunk boundaries for incremental read
    return stream_cached_read(stream, &canonical, &cached, start, count, db).await;
}
// Fall back to cold path (existing tokio::fs::read)
```

2. **New function `stream_cached_read`**:
   - Open `tokio::fs::File::open(&canonical)`
   - For each chunk in [start_chunk, start_chunk + count):
     - Seek to cached offset, read exactly cached length bytes
     - Verify BLAKE3 hash matches cached hash
     - Send BLOCK_HEADER + BLOCK_DATA frames
   - Send TRANSFER_COMPLETE with cached root

3. **The existing cold path remains** as fallback (will be replaced in a later task by streaming chunker).

## TDD Approach

1. Write test: `read_response` with a pre-populated Merkle cache streams chunks without reading the full file
2. Write test: FUSE-like scenario where stat (which populates cache) → read (uses warm path)
3. Write test: Hash verification fails gracefully if cached boundaries are stale (file changed)
4. Use `InMemoryConnection` to verify wire protocol unchanged
5. All existing tests pass

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] When Merkle cache is warm, `read_response` reads chunks individually from disk (no `tokio::fs::read`)
- [ ] Peak memory for warm-path reads is O(max_chunk_size)
- [ ] Wire protocol is unchanged (existing tests pass)
- [ ] Stale cache entries are detected (hash mismatch → fallback)
- [ ] Cold path (cache miss) still works (existing behavior preserved)

## Worktree

```bash
git worktree add ../rift-server-stream-warm phase4-server-stream-warm
```

title: Client streaming: eliminate chunk accumulation and Vec copies
description:
# Client Streaming: Eliminate Chunk Accumulation and Vec Copies

## Problem

The client collects ALL received chunk data into `Vec<ChunkData>` where each `ChunkData.data` is a `Vec<u8>`. For a large file this means the client holds the entire file in memory at once. On top of that, `assemble_byte_range()` concatenates all chunks into another `Vec<u8>` then slices it — double-buffering. The codec layer adds further unnecessary copies.

### Data copy chain (current, per byte):
```
QUIC socket → BytesMut (read_buf) → Vec<u8> (codec copy) → Bytes (recv_frame) → Vec<u8> (ChunkData.data) → Vec<u8> (assemble_byte_range) → Bytes (FUSE reply)
```
That's 4-6 copies per byte.

### Data copy chain (proposed):
```
QUIC socket → BytesMut → Bytes (freeze, zero-copy) → Bytes (ChunkData.data) → Vec<u8> (output, only needed range) → Bytes (FUSE reply)
```
That's 2 copies per byte.

## Key Findings

1. **C7**: `decode_message` in `codec.rs` copies `BytesMut → Vec<u8>` via `.to_vec()`, then `recv_frame` wraps it as `Bytes::from(payload)`. Should return `Bytes` directly via `.freeze()`.
2. **C8**: `ChunkData.data` is `Vec<u8>`, copied from `Bytes` via `.to_vec()`. Should be `Bytes`.
3. **C4**: `read_chunks()` accumulates all chunks into `Vec<ChunkData>` before returning. Should process chunks incrementally.
4. **C5**: `assemble_byte_range()` concatenates all chunk data then slices. Should write only needed bytes directly.
5. **C6**: `ChunkStore::write_chunk()` takes `&[u8]`, buffering full chunk before write. `ChunkStore::read_chunk()` returns `Vec<u8>`.

## Subtasks (in order)

### Step 1: Change decode_message to return Bytes (zero-copy)
- In `protocol/codec.rs`, change `buf.split_to(length).to_vec()` to `buf.split_to(length).freeze()`
- Change return type from `Result<Option<(u8, Vec<u8>)>, CodecError>` to `Result<Option<(u8, Bytes)>, CodecError>`
- Update all codec tests to work with `Bytes` instead of `Vec<u8>`
- **TDD**: Write failing test first that expects `Bytes` return type, then make it pass
- **Worktree**: `git worktree add ../rift-bytes phase1-bytes`

### Step 2: Change ChunkData.data from Vec<u8> to Bytes
- In `client/client.rs`, change `ChunkData { data: Vec<u8> }` to `ChunkData { data: Bytes }`
- Remove `.to_vec()` call in `read_chunks()` — `Bytes` from `recv_frame` is already zero-copy
- Update `verify_chunks_integrity` and `assemble_byte_range` in `view.rs` to work with `Bytes`
- **TDD**: Update `read_chunks` tests and `ChunkData`-related tests first
- **Worktree**: same branch as Step 1

### Step 3: Fix assemble_byte_range to avoid double-buffering
- In `client/view.rs`, rewrite `assemble_byte_range` to allocate only the output size and copy only needed portions from each chunk
- Currently: extends all chunks into `all_data: Vec<u8>` (full-file copy), then slices → another copy
- Proposed: allocate `Vec::with_capacity((end - offset) as usize)`, then `extend_from_slice` only from the needed portion of each chunk
- **TDD**: Existing property tests for byte ranges guide refactor; add test verifying no intermediate full-file allocation
- **Worktree**: `git worktree add ../rift-client-stream phase2-client-stream`

### Step 4: Add write_chunk_from_bytes to ChunkStore
- In `client/cache/chunks.rs`, add `write_chunk_from_bytes(&self, hash: &[u8; 32], data: Bytes) -> io::Result<()>`
- This avoids `&[u8]` → `tokio::fs::write` full-buffer path; `Bytes` can be written via `tokio::io::copy`
- **TDD**: Add test for new method, verify round-trip with `read_chunk`
- **Worktree**: same as Step 3

### Step 5: Add read_chunk_range to ChunkStore
- In `client/cache/chunks.rs`, add `read_chunk_range(&self, hash: &[u8; 32], offset: u64, len: usize) -> io::Result<Option<Vec<u8>>>`
- Uses `File::open` + `seek` + `read_exact` instead of `tokio::fs::read` (full file)
- Particularly beneficial when `reconstruct_range()` only needs a few KB from a large chunk
- **TDD**: Test partial reads from mid-chunk, verify they match full-read slices
- **Worktree**: same as Step 3

### Step 6: Refactor read_chunks to process chunks incrementally
- In `client/client.rs`, add streaming variant of `read_chunks` that takes a callback/channel
- Each chunk received → verify hash → write to cache → notify FUSE layer
- FUSE can serve data as soon as the first relevant chunk arrives
- **TDD**: Mock-based tests verifying chunks processed one-at-a-time; test that cache contains each chunk after receipt
- **Worktree**: `git worktree add ../rift-client-streaming phase5-client-streaming`

### Step 7: Optimize recv_frame read buffer
- In `transport/quic.rs`, change `recv_frame` to: parse header first (2-6 bytes needed), then `read_chunk(claimed_length)` for payload
- This avoids 8KB-read-loop for large frames AND avoids over-reading
- **TDD**: Existing transport tests; add test for large (1MB+) frame receive with minimal read calls
- **Worktree**: same as Step 6 or separate

## Dependencies

- Steps 1-2 must come first (zero-copy foundation enables later steps)
- Step 3 depends on Step 2 (assemble_byte_range needs Bytes ChunkData)
- Steps 4-5 can be done in parallel with Step 3
- Step 6 depends on Steps 1-5 (needs Bytes-based pipeline and cache)

## Architecture Note

The `ShareView::read()` trait currently returns `Vec<u8>`. Long-term, it should return `Bytes` or a lazy handle that reads from cached chunks on demand. But this is a larger API change — the subtasks above can deliver incremental wins without changing the trait.

title: Server streaming: eliminate full-file Vec<u8> reads
description:
# Server Streaming: Eliminate Full-File Vec<u8> Reads (REVISED)

## Current Status
Several subtasks were previously marked as closed, but a review of the implementation reveals that the core memory goal (peak memory O(max_chunk_size)) has NOT been achieved in the cold paths. 

The current implementations of 'streaming' paths still perform full-file passes using a flawed Chunker::chunk_stream() that only yields boundaries, necessitating subsequent full-file reads or, in some cases, loading all chunk data into memory.

## Revised Goal
The goal remains to eliminate all O(file_size) memory allocations during server-side read operations.

## Revised Subtasks

### Step 1: FIX Streaming Chunker API (Chunker::chunk_stream) [RE-OPENED]
- FIX: Change chunk_stream to actually yield a Stream of (offset, length, data) instead of collecting all boundaries into a Vec.
- This is the critical-path enabler for all subsequent streaming tasks.

### Step 2: Warm-Path Streaming Read [STABLE]
- Use cached chunk boundaries to read and stream chunks individually.
- Current implementation in handler/read.rs::stream_cached_read meets the O(max_chunk_size) goal.

### Step 3: Cold-Path Streaming Read [RE-OPENED]
- Replace current cold-path implementation with a true single-pass streaming process.
- Use the fixed Chunker::chunk_stream to read-hash-send each chunk immediately.
- Build the Merkle tree from the stream of hashes.

### Step 4: Refactor merkle_drill_response to use true streaming [RE-OPENED]
- Replace build_and_cache_tree with a true streaming variant that avoids full-file reads and O(file_size) materialization.

### Step 5: Refactor merkle_cache to use true streaming [RE-OPENED]
- Replace the current compute_file_merkle_tree (which loads all chunks into memory) with a true streaming implementation.

### Step 6: Optimize send_frame [OPEN]
- Eliminate per-frame BytesMut allocation in transport/quic.rs.

## Architecture Note
The wire protocol remains unchanged. The primary focus is on memory efficiency on the server.

Peak memory must be O(max_chunk_size) approx 512 KB regardless of file size.
close_reason: All 6 children complete; peak memory O(max_chunk_size) achieved in all server read paths

title: Streaming read pipeline: eliminate Vec<u8> gathering anti-patterns
description:
# Streaming Read Pipeline: Eliminate Vec<u8> Gathering Anti-Patterns

## Problem

The server reads entire files into `Vec<u8>` with `tokio::fs::read()` before streaming a single byte to the client. The client collects all chunks into memory simultaneously. For a 2 GB file, peak memory is ~4 GB (2 GB server + 2 GB client). It should be ~1 MB (2 × 512 KB chunk buffers).

The protocol wire format (BLOCK_HEADER + BLOCK_DATA frames over QUIC streams) is **already streaming-friendly** — no protocol changes needed.

## Key Findings

See investigation reports in the repo root:
- INVESTIGATION_SERVER.md — Server-side analysis (3 CRITICAL: tokio::fs::read in read.rs, drill.rs, merkle_cache.rs)
- INVESTIGATION_CLIENT.md — Client-side analysis (ChunkData accumulation, assemble_byte_range double-buffering, codec copies)
- INVESTIGATION_PROTOCOL.md — Protocol/common analysis (Chunker API requires &[u8], Blake3Hash lacks streaming, codec copies)
- INVESTIGATION_CONSOLIDATED.md — Merged summary with action plan

## Sub-epics

1. **Server streaming** (read.rs, drill.rs, merkle_cache.rs, crypto.rs) — Replace full-file reads with incremental streaming
2. **Client streaming** (client.rs, view.rs, codec.rs, chunks.rs) — Eliminate chunk accumulation and unnecessary copies

## Approach

All changes follow **TDD** and use **git worktrees** for isolation. Each subtask has tests written first.

title: Hybrid cache+network chunk fetching in read() — eliminate all-or-nothing cache policy
description:
The current read() path has two separate worlds: serve ENTIRELY from cache, or fetch ENTIRELY from the server. The manifest_covers_range() gate requires all chunks from index 0 to be present in metadata, and reconstruct_range() requires all chunk data files to exist on disk. Missing even one chunk evicts the manifest and re-fetches everything.

Better architecture: for each chunk in the requested range, try cache first, then fetch from network if missing. Once all chunks are available, reconstruct the range.

Proposed read() flow:
1. Look up manifest → get chunk metadata for the range
2. For each chunk in [start_chunk, end_chunk]:
   - Is data in cache? → use it
   - If not → add to 'missing' set
3. Fetch missing contiguous ranges from server (minimizing round trips)
4. Cache newly fetched chunks and upsert manifest
5. Reconstruct range from collected chunks (now all available)

This eliminates:
- manifest_covers_range as a from-chunk-0 gate (becomes: does manifest have metadata for the needed chunks?)
- The 'evict manifest, re-fetch everything' path
- The performance regression of re-fetching already-cached chunks

The server's read_chunks API takes (handle, start_chunk, chunk_count) for a contiguous range. So the implementation should compute minimal missing sub-ranges and fetch each in one round trip:
```
Example: need chunks [0,1,2,3,4,5], chunks 2-3 are cached
  → missing: [0,1] and [4,5]
  → fetch (start=0, count=2) and (start=4, count=2)
  → reconstruct from all 6 chunks
```

Depends on: the manifest must store complete leaf list (already done for resolved.leaves).
Blocks: the current all-or-nothing design causes unnecessary re-fetches for partial cache hits.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:

Primary file: crates/rift-client/src/view.rs — the read() method (lines ~280-480)

Current architecture (two separate worlds):
- Cache-hit path (line ~315-340): manifest_covers_range() gates the entire read. If manifest metadata is complete from chunk 0 and all chunk data files exist on disk, serve from cache. If ANY chunk is missing (data file on disk), evict manifest and fall through to network path.
- Network path (line ~390-475): Fetch ALL chunks in the requested range from server, verify, cache, assemble. No attempt to use already-cached chunks.

Key data structures:
- Manifest (db.rs): Vec<ChunkInfo> with {index, offset, length, hash} for EVERY chunk in the file. Built from resolved.leaves, always complete.
- ChunkStore (chunks.rs): Content-addressable key-value store. chunk_exists(hash) checks if data file is on disk.
- FileCache (db.rs): Owns both SQLite (metadata) and ChunkStore (data). has get_manifest(), put_chunk(), get_chunk(), reconstruct_range().

Server API: read_chunks(handle, start_chunk, chunk_count) — fetches a contiguous range of chunks in one QUIC stream/round-trip.

Proposed new read() flow:

```
1. Compute start_chunk, end_chunk from offset/length (same as now)
2. Look up manifest → if exists, get chunk metadata for [start_chunk..end_chunk]
3. For each chunk in the range:
   a. Check chunk_exists(hash) in ChunkStore
   b. If exists → read from cache
   c. If missing → add to missing set
4. Compute minimal contiguous fetch ranges from missing set:
   e.g., need [0,1,2,3,4,5], cached [2,3] → fetch [0,1] and [4,5]
5. For each missing range: read_chunks(handle, start, count) from server
   - Verify each chunk's hash, length, and Merkle cross-reference
   - Store fetched chunks in ChunkStore (put_chunk)
   - Upsert manifest (put_manifest)
6. Reconstruct range from ALL collected chunks (cache + network)
```

Changes required:
- view.rs read(): Major restructure — merge cache-hit and network-fetch into a single flow
- view.rs: manifest_covers_range() can be simplified or removed (replace with: does manifest have metadata for needed range?)
- view.rs: try_read_from_cache() may be removed or reworked (offline-only path still needs consideration)
- db.rs: Add a method to check which chunks in a range have data on disk (e.g., missing_chunks_in_range())
- No changes to chunks.rs or the protocol

This is the single most impactful cache improvement after the current branch merges. It eliminates the all-or-nothing policy that causes re-fetching already-cached data.

title: Add info!() tracing for every network transfer in the read pipeline
description:
## Goal

Add structured `info!()` tracing spans and events for every network transfer in
the read pipeline. Currently there is minimal logging: `view.rs` has a few
`debug!()` calls for cache hits/misses, but nothing at the transfer level. When
debugging the recent subtree-skipping bug, we had to compare files byte-by-byte
because there was no logging to tell us what the client requested or received.

## Tracing points

### Server side (`crates/rift-server/src/handler/`)

#### `stat.rs` — stat request
```
INFO stat_request: handle={handle}, path={canonical}
INFO stat_response: handle={handle}, size={size}, root_hash={hash[..8]}, cached={bool}
```
- `cached`: whether the merkle root came from the DB cache or was computed fresh

#### `drill.rs` — merkle drill request
```
INFO merkle_drill_request: handle={handle}, hash={hash[..8]}, resolve_leaves={bool}
INFO merkle_drill_response: handle={handle}, parent_hash={hash[..8]}, children={count}, leaves={count}, subtrees={count}, depth={depth}
```
- `children`: total number of children returned
- `leaves`: how many are leaf nodes
- `subtrees`: how many are subtree nodes
- This is critical for diagnosing: "is the server returning subtrees that the client doesn't resolve?"

#### `read.rs` — chunk transfer
```
INFO read_request: handle={handle}, path={canonical}, start_chunk={start}, chunk_count={count}
INFO read_transfer_start: handle={handle}, total_chunks={count}, file_size={size}
INFO read_transfer_complete: handle={handle}, root_hash={hash[..8]}, chunks_sent={count}, bytes_sent={total_bytes}
```
- `start_chunk` and `chunk_count`: what the client asked for
- `chunks_sent` and `bytes_sent`: what was actually sent
- This lets you verify the server is sending the right number of chunks

### Client side (`crates/rift-client/src/`)

#### `client.rs` — read_chunks
```
INFO read_chunks_request: handle={handle}, start_chunk={start}, chunk_count={count}
INFO read_chunks_response: handle={handle}, chunks_received={count}, total_bytes={bytes}, merkle_root={hash[..8]}
INFO read_chunks_chunk: handle={handle}, chunk_index={idx}, length={len}, hash={hash[..8]}, hash_valid={bool}
```
- `hash_valid`: whether `blake3(chunk_data) == chunk.hash` (after bead rift-6ocb adds verification)
- Per-chunk logging should be at `debug!()` level to avoid log spam for large files,
  but the overall `read_chunks_response` should be `info!()`

#### `client.rs` — merkle_drill
```
INFO merkle_drill_request: handle={handle}, hash={hash[..8]}
INFO merkle_drill_response: handle={handle}, parent_hash={hash[..8]}, children={count}, leaves={count}, subtrees={count}
```

#### `view.rs` — overall read orchestration
```
INFO read_start: path={path}, offset={offset}, length={length}, handle={handle}
INFO read_drill_resolved: handle={handle}, total_leaves={count}, subtrees_expanded={count}, depth={depth}
INFO read_complete: path={path}, offset={offset}, length={length}, bytes_returned={bytes}, root_hash_valid={bool}
INFO read_hash_mismatch: path={path}, expected_root={hash[..8]}, got_root={hash[..8]}, level={level}
```
- `subtrees_expanded`: how many subtree drill calls were needed
- `root_hash_valid`: whether the reconstructed root matches stat's root_hash
- `read_hash_mismatch`: emitted at `error!()` level when any verification fails

## Implementation guidelines

1. **Use `tracing::instrument`** with `skip_all` for performance-sensitive paths,
   adding explicit fields via `fields()`:
   ```rust
   #[instrument(skip_all, fields(
       handle = %handle,
       start_chunk = start_chunk,
       chunk_count = chunk_count,
   ))]
   async fn read_chunks(...) { ... }
   ```

2. **Hash abbreviations**: Never log full 32-byte hashes. Always truncate to
   first 8 bytes (16 hex chars) using `{hash[..8]}`. This is enough to identify
   a specific hash while keeping logs readable.

3. **Level policy**:
   - `info!()`: transfer-level events (start, complete, error)
   - `debug!()`: per-chunk events, cache hits/misses
   - `error!()`: hash verification failures, protocol violations
   - `trace!()`: frame-level details (only if RUST_LOG=trace)

4. **Span structure**: Each network request should be a span, not just events:
   ```rust
   let span = tracing::info_span!("read_chunks", handle = %handle);
   let _enter = span.enter();
   // ... all events within this span are grouped
   ```

5. **Performance**: Hash verification adds CPU cost per chunk. The `info!()` macro
   already zero-costs when the level is disabled. But computing the hash for
   the log field must only happen when the level is enabled:
   ```rust
   if tracing::info_enabled!() {
       tracing::info!(hash_valid = blake3(&chunk.data) == chunk.hash);
   }
   ```
   Or defer: since hash verification is now required (rift-6ocb), the cost is
   already paid. Just log the result.

## Example log output for a successful 80MB file read

```
INFO read_start: path=/vmlinux, offset=0, length=131072, handle=0192...
INFO merkle_drill_request: handle=0192..., hash=<empty>
INFO merkle_drill_response: handle=0192..., parent_hash=a8d4..., children=7, leaves=0, subtrees=7
INFO read_drill_resolved: handle=0192..., total_leaves=412, subtrees_expanded=7, depth=2
INFO read_chunks_request: handle=0192..., start_chunk=0, chunk_count=1
INFO read_transfer_complete: handle=0192..., root_hash=a8d4..., chunks_sent=1, bytes_sent=524288
INFO read_chunks_response: handle=0192..., chunks_received=1, total_bytes=524288, merkle_root=a8d4...
INFO read_complete: path=/vmlinux, offset=0, length=131072, bytes_returned=131072, root_hash_valid=true
```

## Example log output for a hash mismatch

```
ERROR read_hash_mismatch: path=/vmlinux, expected_root=a8d49472..., got_root=00000000..., level=0
ERROR read_chunks_chunk: handle=0192..., chunk_index=3, hash_valid=false
```

## Affected files

| File | Changes |
|------|---------|
| `crates/rift-server/src/handler/stat.rs` | Add `info!()` on request/response |
| `crates/rift-server/src/handler/drill.rs` | Add `info!()` on request/response |
| `crates/rift-server/src/handler/read.rs` | Add `info!()` on transfer start/complete |
| `crates/rift-client/src/client.rs` | Add `info!()`/`debug!()` in `read_chunks`, `merkle_drill` |
| `crates/rift-client/src/view.rs` | Add `info!()` in `read()`, `resolve_merkle_tree()` |

## Dependencies

- Should be done after or alongside bead rift-6ocb (hash verification is
  referenced in the logging — the `hash_valid` field only exists after
  verification is implemented)

title: Property test: round-trip every file through the server+client pipeline
description:
## Goal

Write a property-based test that generates random files, serves them through a real
rift server, reads them back through a real rift client, and verifies the data is
identical. This test should catch any remaining bugs in the chunking, merkle tree,
handle mapping, and read paths — including the subtree-skipping bug (rift-6ocb).

## Why property testing

The bug that motivated this bead (skipping subtrees in merkle_drill) only
manifests for files with > 64 chunks. Unit tests typically test small files.
Property tests generate a wide range of sizes, including files that produce
2-level and 3-level merkle trees, catching these boundary cases automatically.

## Test infrastructure

### Test chunker

Use the test chunker parameters `Chunker::new(64, 256, 1024)` (min=64, avg=256,
max=1024 bytes). With these parameters:
- A 256-byte file has ~1 chunk (1 level)
- A 16 KB file has ~64 chunks (boundary: 1 ↔ 2 levels)
- A 65 KB file has ~256 chunks (2 levels)
- A 4 MB file has ~16K chunks (3 levels)

These trigger all Merkle tree depths with reasonable file sizes.

### Server setup

Reuse the existing `start_server()` helper from `crates/rift-client/tests/common.rs`.
Use `NoopCache` for the merkle cache (simpler, no DB dependency) or the real
`Database::open_in_memory()`.

### Client setup

Use `RiftClient::connect()` or `InMemoryConnection` for direct in-process testing.
For full e2e, use QUIC connections. For faster property tests, use
`InMemoryConnection` (bypasses QUIC entirely).

## Properties to test

### Property 1: Round-trip identity

For any file content `data`:
```
write("testfile", data) on server side
let result = client.read(handle, 0, data.len());
assert_eq!(result, data);
```

This tests that the entire read pipeline (stat → merkle_drill → resolve subtrees
→ read_chunks → hash verification → reassembly) produces byte-identical output.

### Property 2: Random offset reads

For any file content `data` and any offsets `(off, len)` where `off < data.len()`:
```
let result = client.read(handle, off, len);
assert_eq!(result, &data[off..min(off+len, data.len())]);
```

This tests that arbitrary offset/length reads return the correct slice.

### Property 3: Merkle tree consistency

For any file content `data`:
```
let stat_root = stat(handle).root_hash;
let drill = merkle_drill(handle, []);
// Resolve all subtrees recursively
let all_leaves = resolve(drill);
// Verify: blake3(data) should NOT equal root (it's a tree, not a single hash)
// Verify: root should equal merkle_root from stat
// Verify: all leaf hashes should match blake3(chunk_data)
// Verify: root should equal merkle_root from TRANSFER_COMPLETE
```

### Property 4: Merkle verification detects corruption

For any file content `data`:
```
// Corrupt one byte in a chunk's data
let mut result = client.read(handle, 0, data.len());
// Manually mutate chunk data in the transfer → verify hash check fails
```

This is harder to test with a real server (can't inject corruption easily).
Alternative: test the `verify_node` and per-chunk verification in isolation.

### Property 5: Handle consistency

For any file content `data`:
```
let handle1 = stat(handle).handle;
let handle2 = lookup(root_handle, "testfile").handle;
assert_eq!(handle1, handle2);
```

## Input strategies

```rust
proptest! {
    #[test]
    fn prop_roundtrip_file(
        data in proptest::collection::vec(any::<u8>(), 0..2_000_000)
    ) {
        // Generate files from 0 bytes to 2 MB
        // With test chunker (min=64, avg=256, max=1024), this covers:
        //   0 bytes → 0 chunks
        //   1-64 bytes → 1 chunk
        //   100 KB → ~400 chunks (2+ merkle levels)
        //   2 MB → ~8000 chunks (3 merkle levels)
        let rt = tokio::runtime::Runtime::new().unwrap();
        rt.block_on(async_roundtrip(&data));
    }

    #[test]
    fn prop_random_offset_read(
        data in proptest::collection::vec(any::<u8>(), 100..500_000),
        offset in 0usize..500_000,
        length in 1usize..65536,
    ) {
        proptest_assume!(offset < data.len());
        // ...test read at offset
    }
}
```

## Implementation plan

1. **Add a property test module** in `crates/rift-client/tests/prop_roundtrip.rs`

2. **Create a `TestServer` helper** that:
   - Starts a real rift server backed by a temp directory
   - Uses `InMemoryConnection` to bypass QUIC
   - Returns a connected client + the handle database

3. **Write `prop_roundtrip_file`**: Generate random file bytes, write to server's
   share directory, read back through client, compare byte-for-byte.

4. **Write `prop_random_offset_read`**: Same, but test reads at random offsets.

5. **Write `prop_merkle_consistency`**: After reading, verify that merkle hashes
   at every level are consistent (using the new `MerkleTree::verify_node` from
   bead rift-6ocb).

6. **Add to CI**: Run with `proptest-config` cases reasonable for CI (32-64 cases)
   and a more thorough nightly run (1000+ cases).

## Edge cases to ensure coverage

| File size | Expected chunks | Merkle levels | Purpose |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|---------|
| 0 bytes | 0 | 0 | Empty file |
| 1 byte | 1 | 1 | Single-chunk file |
| 64 bytes | 1 | 1 | Min chunk boundary |
| 65 bytes | 1-2 | 1-2 | CDC boundary |
| ~16 KB | ~64 | 1→2 | Fanout boundary (1 level vs 2) |
| ~17 KB | ~65 | 2 | First subtree in merkle drill |
| ~100 KB | ~400 | 2 | Multi-subtree |
| ~4 MB | ~16000 | 3 | Deep merkle tree |
| ~16 MB | ~64000 | 3 | Very large, many subtrees |

## Dependencies

- Bead rift-6ocb must be completed first (the recursive drill + hash verification
  must be in place, otherwise large-file tests will fail)
- `rift-common::crypto::MerkleTree::verify_node` must exist

## Affected files

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `crates/rift-client/tests/prop_roundtrip.rs` | New: property tests |
| `crates/rift-client/tests/common.rs` | Extend: add `InMemoryConnection` server helper |
| `Cargo.toml` (workspace) | Add proptest dependency if not present (already is) |

title: Audit client/server protocol correctness: chunking, handles, merkle tree, serialization
description:
## Goal

Systematic audit of the full data path from server disk to client memory, checking
that every step of the protocol produces consistent results. The recent view.rs bug
(showing that merkle_drill subtree children were silently skipped) highlights that
there may be other inconsistencies hiding in the protocol implementation.

## Areas to investigate

### 1. CDC chunking consistency

**Question**: Does the server chunk a file the same way every time it's read?

The server reads the file from disk on every `stat`, `merkle_drill`, and
`read_chunks` call. If a file is modified between calls, the chunk boundaries
and merkle tree could change mid-transaction.

Check:
- `stat_response` in `crates/rift-server/src/handler/stat.rs` calls
  `get_or_compute_merkle_root` which chunks the file
- `merkle_drill_response` in `crates/rift-server/src/handler/drill.rs` chunks the
  file when the cache misses
- `read_response` in `crates/rift-server/src/handler/read.rs` chunks the file
  every time (note the TODO about caching chunk boundaries)

**Risk**: If the file changes between stat and read, the client has the wrong
chunk_starts table and gets wrong data. The merkle root from stat won't match
the one from TRANSFER_COMPLETE, but only the client can detect this — and
currently it doesn't.

**Investigate**: What happens under concurrent file modifications? Does the
merkle root from stat always match the one from the read?

### 2. Handle database consistency

**Question**: Does `HandleDatabase` return consistent paths for the same handle
across calls?

The `HandleDatabase` (`crates/rift-server/src/handle.rs`) maps UUID handles to
filesystem paths. The paths are resolved via `canonicalize()` on first access
and stored. But if a file is moved/renamed, the stored path becomes stale.

Check:
- Is there a mechanism to detect stale handles?
- Does `resolve()` in `handler/mod.rs` verify the canonical path is still valid?
- What happens if a handle is used after the underlying file is deleted?
- The `NoopCache` used in tests bypasses merkle caching — does the real
  `Database` handle concurrent access correctly?

### 3. Merkle tree cache: server-side DB vs in-memory

**Question**: Do the DB-cached merkle children match what `build_with_cache_and_offsets`
produces?

The `build_and_cache_tree` function in `handler/drill.rs` builds the tree from
the file and caches it. But when the drill handler later retrieves children from
the DB, are the `MerkleChildProto` values byte-identical to what would be
computed fresh?

Check:
- `put_tree` serializes the cache HashMap to the DB. `get_children` deserializes.
- Do the `MerkleChild::Leaf` lengths survive the round-trip? (They're stored
  separately in `leaf_infos`.)
- Are `chunk_index` values preserved?
- Test: build tree → put_tree → get_children → compare with original cache.

### 4. Serialization: proto ↔ Rust type mapping

**Question**: Are there any data losses in the proto serialization round-trip?

Specific areas:
- `MerkleChildProto.length` is `uint64` — no concern
- `MerkleChildProto.chunk_index` is `uint32` — fine for < 4B chunks
- `MerkleChildProto.hash` is `bytes` — must be exactly 32 bytes. Is this validated?
- `MerkleChildProto.child_type` is `MerkleChildType` enum — what happens with
  unknown values? The client uses `== MerkleChildSubtree` comparison; if the
  server sends an unexpected value, the child is treated as a leaf. This could
  lead to incorrect behavior.
- `FileAttrs.root_hash` is `bytes` — empty vs 32 bytes. Is empty handled
  consistently? (View.rs checks `root_hash.is_empty()` and returns Io error)
- `FileAttrs.mode` is `uint32` — Unix permissions fit in 12 bits. Are the upper
  bits masked consistently?
- `FileAttrs.nlinks` is `uint32` — view.rs coerces 0 to 1 for FUSE. Is this applied
  consistently?

### 5. Client ignores TRANSFER_COMPLETE.merkle_root

**Question**: The server sends `TRANSFER_COMPLETE` with the file's merkle root at
the end of every chunk transfer. The client receives it in `read_chunks` and
stores it in `ChunkReadResult.merkle_root`. But `view.rs` never compares it
against the root hash from stat.

Check: After the view.rs fix (bead rift-6ocb), is this comparison added? Yes, it
should be. This investigation is to confirm that the server always sends a
correct merkle_root in TRANSFER_COMPLETE, even when the file was modified between
stat and read.

### 6. Multi-stream interleaving

**Question**: Can two concurrent reads of the same file interleave chunk data
incorrectly?

The QUIC stream is per-request (each `read_chunks` opens a new stream). But the
server reads the file fresh for each stream. If the file changes between two
concurrent reads, they could get different merkle trees. The client would check
hashes from one tree against the other.

Investigate: Is there a mechanism to "pin" a merkle tree version for the
duration of a read?

### 7. Server reads entire file for every chunk request

**Question**: The `read_response` handler in `handler/read.rs` calls
`tokio::fs::read(&canonical)` on every request, then chunks the entire file,
just to serve a few chunks. There's a TODO comment about caching chunk boundaries.

For a 1 GB file, this means reading 1 GB from disk to serve even a single 128 KB
chunk. This is both a performance issue and a correctness risk (TOCTOU).

Investigate: What's the plan for caching chunk boundaries? Is the existing
merkle DB cache sufficient, or does the read handler need its own?

## Deliverable

A document (markdown file in the repo, or a comment on this bead) summarizing:
- Each area: ✅ correct, ⚠️ potential issue, ❌ confirmed bug
- Priority ranking of any issues found
- Recommended fixes or follow-up beads

No code changes required for this bead — pure investigation.

title: Implement readlink RPC and FUSE handler for symlink support
description:
## Problem

The FUSE mount does not support symlinks. When the kernel encounters a symlink inode
(returned from readdir/lookup with `S_IFLNK`), it calls `readlink()` on the FUSE
filesystem. The Rift FUSE implementation never overrides [`PathFilesystem::readlink()`],
so the default implementation returns `ENOSYS`, causing all symlink access to fail.

Empirically confirmed via `rsync -avcn` comparing a kernel source tree against the
FUSE mount: every symlink entry shows as a difference.

## Root Cause

Two-part gap:

1. **Protocol gap**: There is no `Readlink` RPC in `proto/operations.proto`. The server
   resolves symlinks internally (for path traversal) but never sends the link target
   to the client.

2. **FUSE client gap**: `crates/rift-client/src/fuse.rs` does not implement the
   `readlink()` method from the fuse3 `PathFilesystem` trait. The default returns
   `ENOSYS`.

## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Add Readlink RPC to protocol

**File**: `proto/operations.proto`

Add after the existing LOOKUP block (use opcodes 0x22 / 0x23, next available):

```protobuf
// READLINK (0x22 / 0x23)
message ReadlinkRequest {
  bytes handle = 1;  // handle of the symlink
}
message ReadlinkResponse {
  oneof result {
    string      target = 1;  // symlink target path (as stored on disk, can be relative)
    ErrorDetail error  = 2;
  }
}
```

Update `prost-build` in `crates/rift-protocol/build.rs` if needed (it already compiles
`operations.proto`).

### Step 2: Add FrameType variants

**File**: `crates/rift-protocol/src/framing.rs` (or wherever FrameType enum is defined)

Add:
- `ReadlinkRequest = 0x22`
- `ReadlinkResponse = 0x23`

Ensure the From<ReadlinkRequest>/From<ReadlinkResponse> impls wire these correctly.

### Step 3: Server handler — implement readlink

**File**: `crates/rift-server/src/handler/` (new file `readlink.rs` or add to `mod.rs`)

The handler should:
1. Receive `ReadlinkRequest` with a handle.
2. Resolve the handle to its canonical path via `HandleDatabase`.
3. Verify the file is actually a symlink (`symlink_metadata().is_symlink()`).
4. Read the link target with `tokio::fs::read_link(path)`.
5. Return the target string as-is (relative or absolute, as stored on disk).
6. Security: the server already resolves paths with `resolve_path()` which checks
   symlink escapes. For readlink specifically, we just return the *stored* target —
   we do NOT follow the link. The client/kernel will resolve it through further lookups.

```rust
pub async fn handle_readlink(
    share: &Path,
    handle_db: &HandleDatabase,
    req: ReadlinkRequest,
) -> ReadlinkResponse {
    let handle = Uuid::from_bytes(&req.handle);
    let path = match handle_db.get_path(handle).await {
        Some(p) => p,
        None => return ReadlinkResponse { result: Some(Error(stale_handle_error())) },
    };
    match tokio::fs::read_link(&path).await {
        Ok(target) => ReadlinkResponse { result: Some(Ok(target.to_string_lossy().into())) },
        Err(e) => ReadlinkResponse { result: Some(Error(io_error_to_fs_error(e))) },
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Client RemoteShare — add readlink method

**File**: `crates/rift-client/src/client.rs`

Add a `readlink(handle: Uuid) -> Result<String>` method that:
1. Sends a `ReadlinkRequest` with the handle.
2. Receives `ReadlinkResponse`.
3. Returns the target string on success.

### Step 5: ShareView — add readlink to the trait

**Files**: `crates/rift-client/src/view.rs`

Add to `ShareView` trait:
```rust
async fn readlink(&self, path: &Path) -> Result<String, FsError>;
```

Implement in `RiftShareView` by resolving the path to a handle and calling
`remote.readlink(handle)`.

### Step 6: FUSE readlink handler

**File**: `crates/rift-client/src/fuse.rs`

Add the `readlink` override to `RiftFilesystem`:
```rust
async fn readlink(&self, _req: Request, path: &OsStr) -> Result<ReplyData> {
    let rust_path = Path::new(path);
    let target = self.view.readlink(rust_path).await.map_err(to_errno)?;
    Ok(ReplyData { data: Bytes::from(target.into_bytes()) })
}
```

### Step 7: Integration tests

**File**: `crates/rift-server/tests/server.rs` — add test:
- Create a temp share with relative symlinks, absolute symlinks, and chained symlinks
- Call readlink via the handler and verify targets

**File**: `crates/rift-client/tests/client.rs` — add test:
- Spin up server with symlinks, connect client, call `readlink()` and verify targets

## Security Considerations

- `readlink` does NOT follow the link — it only returns the stored target string.
- The server should validate that the handle refers to an actual symlink (return
  `EINVAL` or `ENOTTY` if called on a regular file/dir).
- Existing `resolve_path()` / symlink-escape checks continue to protect path traversal
  for lookup/readdir operations. readlink is informational only.
- Relative symlink targets (e.g., `../foo`) are safe to return as-is — the client
  kernel resolves them relative to the symlink's parent directory.

## Existing Test Coverage

The server already has symlink-related tests:
- `readdir_and_lookup_return_same_handle_for_symlink` (server.rs:773)
- `resolve_rejects_outside_symlink` (server.rs:482)
- `resolve_rejects_intermediate_symlink_escape` (server.rs:512)

These verify handle consistency and escape prevention, but none test returning
the link target to the client.

## Affected Files Summary

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `proto/operations.proto` | Add ReadlinkRequest/ReadlinkResponse |
| `crates/rift-protocol/src/framing.rs` | Add FrameType variants 0x22/0x23 |
| `crates/rift-server/src/handler/readlink.rs` | New: handler implementation |
| `crates/rift-server/src/handler/mod.rs` | Register handler, route opcode |
| `crates/rift-client/src/client.rs` | Add `readlink()` method to RemoteShare |
| `crates/rift-client/src/remote.rs` | (if separate) Add readlink to trait |
| `crates/rift-client/src/view.rs` | Add `readlink()` to ShareView trait + impl |
| `crates/rift-client/src/fuse.rs` | Add `readlink()` FUSE handler override |
| `crates/rift-server/tests/server.rs` | Add readlink integration test |
| `crates/rift-client/tests/client.rs` | Add readlink end-to-end test |

title: Missing tests: MAX_CHUNK_COUNT early-reject, TOCTOU divergence, readdir escape
description:
Test gaps discovered during code review:
1. No unit test for MAX_CHUNK_COUNT guard in read_response that verifies rejection happens BEFORE filesystem access (moved early in refactor, needs test).
2. No test for TOCTOU path-divergence branch in resolve() where fd_resolved != canonical.
3. No unit test for readdir symlink-escape path (entries pointing outside share).
notes: MAX_CHUNK_COUNT early-reject test added in 6107e04. Remaining: TOCTOU divergence test (task 2), readdir escape test (task 4).

title: read_response double-computes chunk hashes
description: In crates/rift-server/src/handler/read.rs, chunk hashes are computed twice: once in chunks_to_read for transmission, and again in leaf_hashes for the Merkle tree + cache. For a 256-chunk request that is 512 BLAKE3 hashes over the same data. Unnecessary CPU overhead.
notes: Part of code-review remediation plan. See commit c5c114f for items 2+3. Will be fixed via subagent-driven plan at docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-24-fix-code-review-findings.md.
close_reason: Fixed in b901541 — leaf_hashes computed once, chunks_to_read derives from it

title: readdir silently drops uncanonicalizable entries
description: In crates/rift-server/src/handler/readdir.rs, canonicalize failures on entries silently return None via ok()?, dropping broken symlinks and permission-denied entries without trace. This makes debugging hard and could hide security-relevant entries.
notes: Part of code-review remediation plan. See commit c5c114f for items 2+3. Will be fixed via subagent-driven plan at docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-24-fix-code-review-findings.md.

title: readdir allows symlinks to escape share root
description: In crates/rift-server/src/handler/readdir.rs, each directory entry is canonicalized but there is no starts_with(share_canonical) check before creating a handle. A symlink inside the share pointing outside will be assigned a UUID in the HandleDatabase. While resolve() later evicts handles that escape, the external path briefly appears in listings and wastes DB entries.
notes: Part of code-review remediation plan. See commit c5c114f for items 2+3. Will be fixed via subagent-driven plan at docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-24-fix-code-review-findings.md.

title: Split handler.rs 1169-line monolith into per-operation modules
Refactor Arc<Option<Database>> to trait object — eliminate double-unwrap anti-pattern
[security] No input validation on start_chunk / chunk_count in READ_REQUEST
[security] xattr handle forgery — unauthenticated reads of user.rift.handle enable injection attacks
close_reason: Fixed: xattr handles now authenticated with HMAC-SHA256. Forged handles without valid signature are rejected and regenerated. Added user.rift.handle.sig xattr (32 bytes). Signing key generated per HandleDatabase instance.

title: [security] TOCTOU symlink race in resolve() — path can change between canonicalize and file operation
close_reason: Fixed: resolve() now returns ResolvedPath with TOCTOU mitigation. On Linux, re-canonicalizes via /proc/self/fd/N after opening the file to detect symlink swap races. Rejects requests where the fd resolves outside the share.

title: whoami() and discover() removed from RiftClient with no replacement
description: The whoami() and discover() methods were deleted from RiftClient in the delta-sync branch. These were used for service discovery — querying what shares a server offers. If anything depends on them, it's now broken. No issue was filed for this removal. Investigate whether anything depends on these methods. If not needed, document why. If needed, restore them.

comments.author: Bogdan Agica
comments.text:
Re-evaluated: whoami()/discover() methods still exist in client code. The actual bug is that the server has no handler for WHOAMI_REQUEST — handle_stream has no match arm for it, so it falls through to ERROR_RESPONSE.

Marking blocked on rift-3lh: implementing WHOAMI properly requires:
- fingerprint/common_name from QUIC TLS client cert (rift-9k6 → rift-3lh)
- available_shares filtered by client cert ACL (rift-5dt → rift-3lh)

Do not implement until auth chain is in place.

title: .unwrap() panic on duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH) in merkle_drill handler
description: handler.rs line 725: duration_since(std::time::UNIX_EPOCH).unwrap() will panic if file mtime is before Unix epoch (corrupted FS, certain NFS mounts). Other handlers correctly use .map(|d| d.as_nanos() as u64).unwrap_or(0). Fix: replace .unwrap() with .unwrap_or_default(). Same pattern exists in merkle.rs line 37 and line 223.
close_reason: Fixed: replaced unwrap() with unwrap_or(0) pattern in merkle.rs:36

title: Full file read + tree rebuild on every MerkleDrill request
description: merkle_drill_response() reads the ENTIRE file into memory, chunks it, builds the full Merkle tree, stores it in DB, and THEN queries the DB — on every single drill request, including subtree drills on the same file. For a 10MB file: 10MB read, hash all chunks, build tree, dozens of SQLite inserts — just to answer a single hash query. Fix: check DB first (like get_or_compute_merkle_root() does). Only rebuild if cache miss or mtime changed. The DB get_children() should be the first operation, not the last. Also test concurrent drill requests on the same file for race conditions on DB writes.
notes:
[Code Review Context — additional duplication paths]

The original bead covers the merkle_drill_response path. A code quality review identified additional Merkle duplication paths that should be in scope:

1. stat_response -> get_or_compute_merkle_root() computes the full Merkle tree, then read_response on the same file computes it AGAIN independently. A typical client workflow (stat then read) doubles the work for the same file.

2. Within read_response itself, the Merkle tree is computed TWICE: once for chunk boundaries/hashes (lines ~590-610), and again at the end for cache storage (lines ~680-700). The leaf_hashes computation is identical both times — it should be computed once and reused.

3. get_or_compute_merkle_root() is called from both stat and lookup, and independently from read_response. There is no sharing of computed results between these call paths within the same request or across concurrent requests.

Proposed additional scope for this bead:
- Extract a compute_merkle_tree() function that returns both root hash and full tree structure
- Use it in stat, lookup, and read paths (eliminates the stat->read double computation)
- In read_response, compute leaf_hashes once and reuse for both chunk sending and cache storage
- Optional: add a small LRU cache keyed by (path, mtime) for very-recently-computed trees
close_reason: Fixed: merkle_drill_response now checks DB cache first (get_merkle for root drill, get_children for subtree drill). Only reads file and rebuilds tree on cache miss. Extracts build_and_cache_tree() helper.

title: Implement server merkle_drill handler using hash lookup

title: Fix O(n²) chunk offset computation in read_response send loop
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handler.rs, read_response builds chunks_to_read as Vec<(usize, usize, Blake3Hash)> containing (chunk_index, chunk_length, hash). It deliberately drops the byte offset.

Then in the send loop (line ~594):

    for (idx, length, hash) in chunks_to_read {
        let start_offset = chunk_boundaries[0..idx]
            .iter()
            .map(|(_, l)| *l)
            .sum::<usize>();   // O(n) per chunk → O(n²) total
        let chunk_data = &content[start_offset..start_offset + length];

The byte offset for chunk idx is already stored in chunk_boundaries[idx].0 (chunk_boundaries is Vec<(offset, length)>). The sum is a slow recomputation of something that's immediately available.

Fix (one line):
1. Change chunks_to_read to include the offset: map to (start + i, *offset, *length, hash) in the collection step (~line 576).
2. Use the stored offset directly in the send loop: let chunk_data = &content[offset..offset + length];
3. Remove the sum computation entirely.

This is a trivial change with no behaviour difference — just eliminates the quadratic work. Matters for files with many small FastCDC chunks.

title: Canonicalize entry paths in readdir_response before storing in HandleDatabase
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handler.rs readdir_response (line ~300), directory entries are stored in the handle database via:

    let entry_path = entry.path();  // tokio::fs::DirEntry::path()
    handle_db.get_or_create_handle(&entry_path).await

entry.path() returns dir_canonical.join(entry.file_name()). For regular files this is fine because dir_canonical is already canonical. But if entry_name is itself a symlink, entry_path is not fully canonical (it contains the symlink name, not the target).

Consequence: lookup_response canonicalizes the path before calling get_or_create_handle (line ~221: tokio::fs::canonicalize(&child_path)), so lookup and readdir can produce *different* UUIDs for the same physical file when that file is a symlink inside the share. The client then has two handles pointing to the same inode, causing cache misses and potentially duplicate entries.

Fix:
1. In the readdir_response stream loop (handler.rs ~line 295-310), after computing entry_path, canonicalize it:
    let entry_canonical = tokio::fs::canonicalize(&entry_path).await;
   Handle the Err case by skipping the entry (same as the existing .ok()? pattern).
2. Use entry_canonical for get_or_create_handle and for the ReaddirEntry handle bytes.
3. Add a test: create a directory with a symlink inside, call readdir then lookup on the symlink name, assert both return the same handle UUID.

Note: this adds one canonicalize syscall per directory entry. For large directories this is noticeable. If that's a concern, limit canonicalization to entries whose file_type is Symlink (already available from entry.file_type()).

Related: this was the same class of macOS path bug fixed in the Merkle cache tests (commit fixing /var vs /private/var).

title: stat_response silently drops handles with invalid ULID size
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handler.rs, stat_response uses filter_map to parse incoming handle bytes into Ulid. When Ulid::from_bytes returns None (e.g. wrong size), that handle is silently dropped from the response. A client sending N handles gets back fewer than N StatResults, with no indication of which handles failed or why.

Before this change, every handle produced either a success StatResult or an error StatResult. The new behavior breaks the 1:1 contract.

Fix: Replace filter_map with a regular for loop or map that produces an error StatResult (e.g. ErrorNotFound) when Ulid::from_bytes returns None, preserving the invariant that the response has the same number of results as request handles.
acceptance_criteria: Sending a StatRequest with a non-16-byte handle returns a StatResult with ErrorNotFound (not omission); Sending a mix of valid and invalid handles returns results for all of them; Existing stat tests continue to pass
close_reason: Fixed: stat_response returns ErrorNotFound for invalid handles instead of silently dropping them

title: Fix TOCTOU race in HandleDatabase::get_or_create_handle
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handle.rs, get_or_create_handle does a lookup (self.map.get_ulid) then an insert (self.map.insert) in two separate steps. Between these two calls, a concurrent task can insert the same relative path with a different ULID. When the second insert fails with FsError::Exists, the error propagates as an IO error to the caller instead of returning the winning handle.

This is a correctness bug under concurrency (e.g. two readdir calls for the same directory racing to create handles for the same child).

Fix: When insert returns FsError::Exists, re-lookup the relative path in the map and return the existing ULID instead of propagating the error. This matches the "get_or_create" contract. Add a proptest or concurrent test that exercises two tasks calling get_or_create_handle for the same path simultaneously.
acceptance_criteria: get_or_create_handle never returns an error when the path already exists; A concurrent test confirms idempotent behavior under race conditions; The "insert then re-lookup on Exists" pattern is documented in a comment
close_reason: Fixed BidirectionalMap::insert rollback on partial failure; get_or_create_handle now re-lookups on Err(FsError::Exists) instead of propagating; concurrent test passes
