**Dependencies**: Requires TLS auth (rift-9k6) to be working first.

title: Implement TLS client certificate authentication
description:
Implement TLS client certificate authentication on server side. Server validates client certificates against trusted CA or pinned certs. Reject connections from unauthorized clients.

**Blocked by**: rift-apf (TLS certificate verification)

**Dependencies**: Requires TLS verification (rift-apf) to be in place first.

title: Implement delta sync via Merkle tree block comparison
description:
Implement delta sync - compare client's cached Merkle tree with server's to identify changed blocks. Only transfer changed blocks, not entire file.

**Blocked by**: rift-jdi (CDC chunking), rift-abh (Merkle tree)

**Uses**: MERKLE_COMPARE, MERKLE_DRILL, MERKLE_LEAVES protocol

title: Implement resume validation (fingerprint check: mtime + size)
description: Every resume request (read or write) must carry the original file's fingerprint: mtime + size as recorded when the transfer started. Server compares fingerprint against current file state before accepting resume. If mismatched (file modified out-of-band), reject with FILE_MODIFIED error.

title: Implement resumable transfers (read and write)
description:
Implement resumable transfers - if transfer interrupted (connection drop), client can resume from where it left off. Server retains partial data for resume retention window (default 1 hour).

**Blocked by**: rift-9l7 (CoW write semantics)

**Dependencies**: Requires CoW semantics (rift-9l7) to have consistent file state for resume.

title: Implement mtime+size fast-path validation
description:
Implement fast-path cache validation using mtime + size comparison.

Status (2025-04-20):
- STAT already uses cache via get_or_compute_merkle_root - verified working  
- MERKLE_DRILL level 0 fast-path: DEFERRED until MERKLE_DRILL protocol finalized

Deferred work:
- Add cache check in merkle_drill_response for level 0 only
- Level > 0 should NOT use cache (tree depth varies)
close_reason: mtime+size fast-path validation already implemented in get_merkle() (stale entries auto-invalidated and deleted). Cache-first drill implemented in rift-qn6. mtime+size check for drill lookups delegated to get_merkle() on root hash miss.

title: Implement Content-Defined Chunking (CDC) with FastCDC
description:
Implement FastCDC for content-defined chunking to replace fixed-offset blocks. This ensures delta sync efficiency - inserting/deleting bytes only affects 1-2 chunks near the edit point, not the entire file. Parameters: min=32KB, target=128KB, max=512KB.

**Status**: Core CDC chunking IMPLEMENTED in rift-common/crypto.rs (Chunker struct). Comprehensive unit + property tests pass. Server uses Chunker in Merkle tree computation.

**Remaining work (subtasks)**:
- rift-mr6: Per-share CDC configuration
- rift-ssu: Parallel chunking for large files
close_reason: Core CDC (FastCDC) and BLAKE3 Merkle tree already implemented. Remaining subtasks tracked as separate beads (rift-mr6, rift-ssu).

title: Implement block-level Merkle tree checksums with BLAKE3
description:
Implement block-level Merkle tree checksums with BLAKE3. Each CDC chunk gets a BLAKE3 hash; chunks combined into a 64-ary Merkle tree.

**Blocked by**: rift-jdi (CDC chunking core required for chunk boundaries)
close_reason: Block-level Merkle tree checksums with BLAKE3 already implemented in rift-common/crypto.rs. CDC chunking (rift-jdi) is also done.

title: Implement CoW write semantics (write to temp, fsync, rename)
description:
Implement copy-on-write write semantics: write to temp file on server, fsync, then atomically rename temp over original. Ensures zero write holes - readers see old or new version, never partial data.

**Blocked by**: rift-4ut (write locking with single-writer lock per file)

**Dependencies**: Requires write locking (rift-4ut) to coordinate writes.

title: Implement write locking with single-writer lock per file
description:
Implement single-writer lock per file on server. When client starts a write, acquire exclusive lock. Other clients can still read (CoW semantics). Lock has progress timeout (default 60s).

**Blocked by**: rift-d7v (delta sync via Merkle tree)

**Dependencies**: Needs delta sync (rift-d7v) to identify which blocks changed before locking.

title: Refactor: async-only filesystem helpers
description:
Remove sync versions of canonicalize_within_share and walk_share, keep only async versions.

## Motivation
The codebase runs entirely on async (tokio). Having both sync and async versions of the same functions creates:
- Code duplication
- Maintenance burden (bugs must be fixed twice)
- Confusion about which to use

## Current State
```
// sync version - used in spawn_blocking contexts
pub fn canonicalize_within_share(path: &Path, share: &Path) -> Option<PathBuf>
pub fn walk_share(share: PathBuf, tx: mpsc::Sender<PathBuf>)

// async version - wraps with spawn_blocking
pub async fn canonicalize_within_share_async(...)
pub async fn run_background_check(...)  // spawns blocking internally
```

## Target State
Keep ONLY async versions:

```rust
// Single implementation
pub async fn walk_share_async(share: PathBuf, tx: mpsc::Sender<PathBuf>) {
    // Use tokio::fs::read_dir for async iteration
}

pub async fn canonicalize_within_share_async(path: &Path, share: &Path) -> Option<PathBuf> {
    let canonical = tokio::fs::canonicalize(path).await.ok()?;
    if !is_within_share(&canonical, share) {
        return None;
    }
    fd_recanonicalize_linux_async(&canonical, share).await
}
```

## Steps
1. Write async implementation of walk_share using tokio::fs
2. Update background_check to use walk_share_async directly
3. Delete sync canonicalize_within_share, keep only async version
4. Delete sync walk_share
5. Delete sync fd_recanonicalize_linux, keep only async version
6. Update all callers

## Files
- crates/rift-server/src/security.rs
- crates/rift-server/src/background_check.rs
- crates/rift-server/tests/server.rs (if sync used in tests)

## Testing
- Ensure all tests run with #[tokio::test]
- Verify no spawn_blocking calls remain for filesystem operations

title: Remove put_merkle — no legitimate production use case
description:
put_merkle only inserts into merkle_cache (no tree_nodes, no leaf_info), creating CacheStatus::Incomplete entries by design. It has no production callers — only tests use it to fabricate stale/incomplete states. With leaf_count now in merkle_cache, tests can construct those states by directly inserting rows instead.

Remove:
- Database::put_merkle method
- MerkleCache trait fn put_merkle
- NoopCache impl of put_merkle
- All test callers (replace with direct DB inserts that set leaf_count appropriately)

title: Eliminate mtime_ns == 0 sentinel conflation
description:
mtime_ns uses 0 as a fallback when modified() or duration_since(UNIX_EPOCH) fails. But 0 is also a valid timestamp (1970-01-01). Code that compares mtime cannot distinguish 'real timestamp 0' from 'unknown mtime'.

Preferred fix: change mtime_ns to Option<u64> everywhere — None means unknown, Some(0) means epoch. Propagates through merkle_cache schema (NULL-able column), LeafInfo, put_tree, cache_status, FileInfo. Schema migration: mtime_ns INTEGER NOT NULL -> mtime_ns INTEGER (nullable). All comparison sites must handle None.

Alternative: use u64::MAX sentinel — less pervasive but semantically unclear. Not preferred.
close_reason: Completed: mtime_ns changed to Option<u64> in merged branch mtime-putmerkle

title: Rewrite background check: async stream walk, bounded parallel processing
description:
Replace the current walk_share (sync, spawn_blocking + mpsc channel) with async-walkdir::WalkDir as a Stream, and process files with bounded parallelism using tokio::spawn + Semaphore.

## Architecture

### Stream: async-walkdir::WalkDir

Use async-walkdir::WalkDir as the directory stream. It is natively a Stream<Item = Result<DirEntry, Error>> — no spawn_blocking, no walkdir, no channel. It handles symlink loop detection and permission-denied directories internally.

Filter non-file entries using the built-in .filter() method:
```rust
use async_walkdir::{WalkDir, Filtering};
use futures::StreamExt;

let stream = WalkDir::new(&share).filter(|entry| async move {
    if entry.file_type().is_file() && !entry.file_type().is_symlink() {
        Filtering::Continue
    } else {
        Filtering::Ignore
    }
}).map(|result| {
    result.ok().map(|entry| entry.path().to_path_buf())
}).filter_map(|path| path);  // filter out errors, keep PathBufs
```

This yields PathBuf lazily — no Vec materialization, no channel. The stream only advances when the consumer polls it, which happens when a semaphore permit is available (natural backpressure).

### process_file(path: PathBuf, db: Arc<Database>, chunker: Arc<Chunker>) -> FileOutcome

Called per file yielded by the stream. Returns a FileOutcome enum for aggregation. Does:
1. tokio::fs::canonicalize(path) — skip on failure (debug log, return FileOutcome::Skipped)
2. tokio::fs::metadata(canonical) — skip on failure (debug log, return FileOutcome::Skipped)
3. Verify is_file() — skip non-regular files
4. db.cache_status(canonical, mtime_ns, file_size) — classify
5. If Complete, return FileOutcome::Complete
6. If not Complete, call recompute_file (existing logic)
7. Return appropriate FileOutcome variant

### run_background_check(share, db, chunker) -> Result<BackgroundCheckSummary>

1. Create the stream: WalkDir::new(&share).filter(...).map(...).filter_map(...)
2. pin_mut!(stream)
3. Create Semaphore with concurrency limit (available_parallelism())
4. Create JoinSet<FileOutcome>
5. Loop: stream.next().await → acquire permit → spawn process_file task
   - The await on semaphore.acquire_owned() provides natural backpressure: when all N workers are busy, the main task pauses (and the stream pauses too, since it is lazy)
6. After stream exhausted, drain JoinSet to collect outcomes
7. Aggregate FileOutcomes into BackgroundCheckSummary
8. Return summary

Concurrency limit: std::thread::available_parallelism().unwrap_or(1). Matches CPU core count, appropriate since compute_file_merkle_tree uses spawn_blocking for CPU-heavy hashing.

### FileOutcome enum

```rust
enum FileOutcome {
    Complete,    // cache up-to-date
    Added,       // was Missing, recomputed successfully
    Stale,       // was Stale, recomputed successfully
    Incomplete,  // was Incomplete, recomputed successfully
    Skipped,     // canonicalize/metadata failed
    Error,       // recompute failed
}
```

Added/Stale/Incomplete mean the file was classified as such AND recomputed successfully. Error means classified as non-Complete but recomputation failed. Skipped means could not even classify (canonicalize/metadata failure).

### Changes to Chunker

Add #[derive(Clone)] to Chunker. Three u32 fields — trivially clonable. Wrap in Arc<Chunker> for the background check so each spawned task gets Arc<Chunker>.

### Dependency changes

- Remove: walkdir (no longer needed, **verify no other consumers in the workspace**)
- Add: async-walkdir (for WalkDir stream)
- Keep: tokio (fs, sync::Semaphore, task::JoinSet), futures (StreamExt)

Note: async-walkdir transitively depends on async-fs and blocking. async-fs provides async filesystem primitives by offloading to a thread pool. This is fine — the thread pool is separate from tokio workers.

### What to remove

- walk_share function (entire function, ~40 lines)
- FileInfo struct (path + mtime_ns + file_size no longer needed — stats happen at process time)
- The mpsc channel (tx, rx)
- spawn_blocking call
- YIELD_EVERY constant
- files_since_yield counter + yield_now() logic

### What stays

- recompute_file (unchanged)
- record_status_counts (may be folded into FileOutcome matching)
- BackgroundCheckSummary struct (unchanged)
- All existing tests (they call run_background_check, which now uses the stream internally)

### Testing

- Existing integration tests call run_background_check, should pass as-is
- Add unit tests for the stream + process pipeline:
  - Empty directory → stream yields nothing
  - Nested directories → all regular files yielded
  - Symlinks are filtered out
  - Permission-denied directory is skipped gracefully
  - Bounded parallelism: semaphore limits concurrent tasks

### Edge cases

- **Empty share**: stream yields nothing → JoinSet empty → summary all zeros
- **Symlink to file**: filtered by .filter() → never yielded
- **Symlink to directory**: filtered (async-walkdir does not follow symlinks by default)
- **Symlink cycle**: async-walkdir handles cycle detection internally
- **Permission denied**: async-walkdir skips and yields Err → filtered by filter_map
- **File deleted between walk and process**: canonicalize/metadata fails → FileOutcome::Skipped
- **Panic in task**: JoinSet::join_next() returns Err → counted as FileOutcome::Error

### Dependency

Depends on rift-uatt (Move Merkle computation out of handler module): the rewritten background_check.rs imports compute_file_merkle_tree and cache_computed_tree from metadata::merkle_ops instead of handler::merkle_cache.
acceptance_criteria:
1. walk_share function and FileInfo struct removed
2. No mpsc channel, no spawn_blocking, no YIELD_EVERY
3. walk_share_stream implemented with tokio::fs::read_dir + async_stream::stream!
4. Cycle detection via (dev, ino) HashSet prevents infinite recursion
5. run_background_check uses walk_share_stream + Semaphore + JoinSet with bounded concurrency
6. process_file returns FileOutcome enum (Complete/Added/Stale/Incomplete/Skipped/Error)
7. Summary is aggregated from FileOutcome values after draining JoinSet
8. Chunker derives Clone (used via Arc<Chunker> in spawned tasks)
9. walkdir removed from Cargo.toml IF no other consumers exist
10. async-stream added to Cargo.toml
11. All existing background_check tests pass
12. New unit tests for walk_share_stream (empty dir, nested dirs, symlinks skipped, cycle detection, permission denied)
13. Zero clippy warnings

title: Replace two-pass channel architecture with collect-then-iterate
description:
Replace the current walk_share (sync, spawn_blocking + mpsc channel) with a simpler collect-then-iterate pattern.

Current architecture:
1. walk_share runs on spawn_blocking thread, sends FileInfo {path, mtime_ns, file_size} through bounded mpsc channel
2. Single async consumer reads one FileInfo at a time, processes sequentially, yields every 64 files

Problems:
- Channel is unnecessary complexity for a one-shot startup sweep
- mtime/file_size captured at walk time, not process time — TOCTOU gap between walk and process
- FileInfo struct carries pre-statted data that may be stale by process time
- Channel error handling (receiver dropped, sender dropped) adds edge cases
- Two separate functions (walk_share + handle_file) that must coordinate through a struct

New architecture:
1. spawn_blocking collects Vec<PathBuf> from walkdir (fast — <1s for 26k files)
2. Async loop iterates over paths, calls tokio::fs::canonicalize + tokio::fs::metadata per file (at process time, closing TOCTOU gap)
3. Sequential processing with yield_now() every iteration to avoid starving request handlers
4. No channel, no FileInfo struct, no YIELD_EVERY constant

This eliminates the channel entirely, reduces the code by ~50 lines, and closes the TOCTOU gap.

Do NOT add async-walkdir or async-fs. Keep walkdir and spawn_blocking for the path collection — walkdir is already a dependency, handles symlink loops and permission-denied gracefully, and the collection is too fast to need streaming.
acceptance_criteria:
- walk_share function and FileInfo struct removed
- run_background_check uses spawn_blocking to collect Vec<PathBuf>, then iterates with for loop
- canonicalize and metadata happen per-file on the async side (tokio::fs)
- mtime_ns computed at process time, not walk time (closes TOCTOU gap)
- yield_now() called per iteration to avoid starving request handlers
- No mpsc channel, no YIELD_EVERY constant
- All background_check tests pass, zero clippy warnings
notes: Withdrawn — need parallel processing, not sequential. Will redesign with bounded concurrency instead.

title: Move Merkle computation out of handler module
description:
Move compute_file_merkle_tree, cache_computed_tree, and sentinel_hash_for_non_file from handler/merkle_cache.rs to a new metadata::merkle_ops module. These are pure utilities with no handler dependency — the background check imports them, creating a backwards coupling. get_or_compute_merkle_root stays in handler because it's the handler entry point and uses the MerkleCache trait.

Current locations:
- handler/merkle_cache.rs: compute_file_merkle_tree, cache_computed_tree, sentinel_hash_for_non_file, get_or_compute_merkle_root, classify_file_type
- handler/merkle_cache_trait.rs: MerkleCache trait, NoopCache, type aliases (stays in handler)

After the move:
- metadata/merkle_ops.rs: compute_file_merkle_tree, cache_computed_tree, sentinel_hash_for_non_file
- handler/merkle_cache.rs: get_or_compute_merkle_root, classify_file_type (imports from metadata::merkle_ops)
- handler/merkle_cache_trait.rs: unchanged
- background_check.rs: imports from metadata::merkle_ops instead of handler::merkle_cache

No logic changes — pure file move with import updates.
acceptance_criteria:
- metadata::merkle_ops exists and exports compute_file_merkle_tree, cache_computed_tree, sentinel_hash_for_non_file
- background_check.rs imports from metadata::merkle_ops, not handler::merkle_cache
- handler/merkle_cache.rs imports from metadata::merkle_ops for the moved functions
- handler/merkle_cache_trait.rs is unchanged
- All tests pass, zero clippy warnings

title: mtime_ns == 0 sentinel conflation
description: When a file's mtime cannot be determined (e.g. stat failure on modified()), the code falls back to mtime_ns = 0 via .unwrap_or(0). But mtime_ns = 0 is also a valid timestamp (Unix epoch). This means: (1) Two files with genuinely different mtimes that both fail stat get mtime_ns=0, causing cache key collisions — a file modified at time X might match a stale cache entry that also fell back to 0. (2) A file genuinely modified at epoch +0ns would produce a correct cache entry with mtime_ns=0, but it's indistinguishable from a sentinel. Fix: use Option<u64> or a sentinel value like u64::MAX to represent unknown mtime. If mtime is unknown, the cache entry should be treated as Stale on next check (forcing recompute).
acceptance_criteria:
- mtime_ns=0 no longer conflated with unknown-mtime
- Unknown mtime produces a distinct sentinel (e.g. u64::MAX) or is handled via Option
- cache_status treats sentinel-mtime entries as Stale (forces recompute on next access)
- All 5 sites that fallback to 0 are updated

title: Share-containment canonicalization helper
description: Consolidate all share-containment/canonicalization logic into a single helper. Currently the codebase has: is_within_share() and fd_recanonicalize_linux() in handler/mod.rs, canonicalize_share_root() as a standalone function, and walk_share uses canonicalize() but no containment check (security gap). These should be unified into one module with clear semantics: canonicalize a path, verify it stays within the share root, and return the canonical path or an error.
acceptance_criteria:
- Single module/function that all share-containment checks go through
- walk_share uses it (currently has a security gap: no containment check after canonicalize)
- handler code uses it instead of scattered is_within_share/canonicalize_share_root calls
- fd_recanonicalize_linux folded in or documented why it stays separate

title: Simplify background check to single-pass with fresh stat
description:
The background check walks the filesystem and captures mtime_ns/file_size in a FileInfo struct during the walk, then checks cache_status with that captured metadata during async processing. By the time a file is processed, the metadata may be stale (file changed between walk and processing) — causing unnecessary Stale recomputes.

Additionally, the two-phase design (walk + process) has a TOCTOU race between delete_merkle and put_tree, causing UNIQUE constraint violations that leave the cache in an Incomplete state (see rift-6s3t).

Change the design:
- Walk sends only PathBuf (not FileInfo) through the MPSC channel
- handle_file stats the file fresh (getting current mtime/size) right before calling cache_status
- This eliminates stale metadata and reduces the FileInfo struct to just PathBuf

This depends on rift-6s3t (put_tree atomicity) being fixed first, since the concurrent-write race is the same root cause.

## Context
Code review findings #10 and #11 from bg-check PR.
acceptance_criteria: FileInfo struct contains only PathBuf. mtime_ns and file_size are obtained fresh at processing time. No unnecessary Stale recomputes from stale walk-time metadata.

title: Move shared Merkle helpers out of handler module
description:
compute_file_merkle_tree and cache_computed_tree live in handler/merkle_cache.rs and are imported by background_check.rs. This creates a dependency from a top-level startup task to handler internals, inverting the module dependency graph.

Plan:
1. Create crates/rift-server/src/metadata/merkle_ops.rs
2. Move compute_file_merkle_tree and cache_computed_tree from handler/merkle_cache.rs into it
3. Re-export from handler/merkle_cache.rs for backward compatibility (pub use)
4. Update background_check.rs to import from metadata::merkle_ops directly
5. Add pub mod merkle_ops to metadata/mod.rs

The functions are pure file-I/O + crypto + DB operations — they have no handler-specific logic (no RequestContext, no response framing). They belong in the metadata layer alongside the DB access code.

## Context
Code review finding #16 from bg-check PR.
acceptance_criteria: background_check.rs imports from metadata::merkle_ops, not handler::merkle_cache. handler/merkle_cache.rs re-exports for its own use. All tests pass.

title: Background pre-caching on server boot
description:
Server currently starts accepting connections with zero cached state. Every first request is a cold cache miss. HandleDatabase::populate_from_share() exists but is never called.

Fix: After binding and creating the Database, spawn a background task that walks the share directory and pre-computes Merkle trees for all files. This eliminates first-access latency for pre-cached files.

Implementation:
1. Add pre_cache_share() function that walks share dir, calls get_or_compute_merkle_root() for each file
2. Spawn as tokio::spawn background task after accept_loop starts
3. Add config options: pre_cache.enabled (bool, default true), pre_cache.max_threads (usize, default 2)
4. Consider ionice for the pre-cache thread pool on Linux
5. Log progress (files cached, elapsed time)

The pre-cache task should:
- Skip files that are already in cache (get_merkle returns Some with matching mtime)
- Use tokio::task::spawn_blocking for CPU-bound hashing
- Be cancellable on shutdown (tokio CancellationToken or similar)

~100 lines. Medium impact — eliminates first-access latency entirely.

title: Cold-path: wrap CPU-bound work in spawn_blocking
description:
BLAKE3 hashing and CDC chunking are CPU-bound operations running directly on the tokio runtime. This blocks the async executor and starves other connections. For large files, this can stall the entire server for seconds.

Fix: Wrap the cold-path computation (chunk_stream + hash + build_tree) in tokio::task::spawn_blocking(). This moves CPU work off the async runtime onto a blocking thread pool, allowing the server to process other connections concurrently.

Note: spawn_blocking runs sync code — cannot use async I/O inside it. Options:
1. Do all file I/O (read chunk data) before entering spawn_blocking, pass Vec<u8> data in
2. Use std::fs (sync I/O) inside spawn_blocking instead of tokio::fs
3. Hybrid: async read into buffer, then spawn_blocking for hashing only

Recommended approach: With the single-pass fix (rift-1cjc), use AsyncStreamCDC::read_chunk() async, then spawn_blocking for the Merkle tree construction. Or do the entire cold path in spawn_blocking using sync I/O since there's no pipelining benefit.

~30 lines. Medium impact for concurrent workloads.

title: Client transport: optimize recv_frame to use header-then-payload read pattern
Client cache: add read_chunk_range for partial chunk reads
Client cache: add write_chunk_from_bytes for zero-copy network→disk
description:
# Client Cache: Add write_chunk_from_bytes for Zero-Copy Network→Disk

## What

Add `ChunkStore::write_chunk_from_bytes(&self, hash: &[u8; 32], data: Bytes) -> io::Result<()>` to `crates/rift-client/src/cache/chunks.rs` that accepts a `Bytes` directly, avoiding the `&[u8]` → `tokio::fs::write` full-buffer path.

## Why

Currently `write_chunk` takes `&[u8]` and calls `tokio::fs::write(&tmp_path, data)`, which copies the entire data slice into a kernel buffer. When `ChunkData.data` is `Bytes` (from previous task), we can write it more efficiently:

1. `Bytes` is reference-counted — no copy needed to pass it around
2. `tokio::fs::write` takes `&[u8]` anyway, but having a `Bytes`-accepting API makes the caller path cleaner and avoids confusion about ownership
3. Future optimization: use `tokio::io::copy` from a `Bytes` cursor to the file for scatter-gather writes

## How

### In `crates/rift-client/src/cache/chunks.rs`:

```rust
use bytes::Bytes;

impl ChunkStore {
    /// Write a chunk from a Bytes object (zero-copy reference, cheap to clone).
    /// Same atomic-write semantics as write_chunk (temp file + rename).
    pub async fn write_chunk_from_bytes(&self, hash: &[u8; 32], data: Bytes) -> io::Result<()> {
        self.write_chunk(hash, &data).await  // delegates to existing impl
    }
}
```

Initially this is a thin wrapper. The optimization is that callers don't need to convert `Bytes` to `&[u8]` (which is trivial, but the API signals that `Bytes` is the preferred type).

### Also update `FileCache::put_chunk`:

```rust
pub async fn put_chunk_bytes(&self, hash: &[u8; 32], data: Bytes) -> SqliteResult<()> {
    // Same as put_chunk but accepts Bytes
    self.write_chunk_from_bytes(hash, data).await...
}
```

## TDD Approach

1. Write test: `write_chunk_from_bytes` then `read_chunk` produces same data
2. Write test: `write_chunk_from_bytes` is idempotent (same as `write_chunk`)
3. Write test: `Bytes`-based write produces same on-disk file as `&[u8]`-based write

## Dependencies

- Requires `ChunkData.data` to be `Bytes` (rift-b0er.2.2) for caller benefit

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `write_chunk_from_bytes` added to `ChunkStore`
- [ ] `put_chunk_bytes` added to `FileCache`
- [ ] Round-trip test: write bytes → read returns same data
- [ ] All existing chunk tests pass

## Worktree

```bash
git worktree add ../rift-client-stream phase2-client-stream
```

title: Server transport: optimize send_frame to eliminate per-frame BytesMut allocation
description:
# Server Transport: Optimize send_frame to Eliminate Per-Frame BytesMut Allocation

## What

In `transport/quic.rs`, `QuicStream::send_frame` allocates a fresh `BytesMut` per frame to encode the varint header + payload before writing. For a read operation sending 1,000 chunks, that's 1,000 small allocations. Replace with either a reusable buffer or separate header/payload writes.

## Why

The current code:
```rust
async fn send_frame(&mut self, type_id: u8, payload: &[u8]) -> Result<(), TransportError> {
    let mut buf = BytesMut::new();
    codec::encode_message(type_id, payload, &mut buf)?;
    self.send.write_all(&buf).await?;
    Ok(())
}
```

For BLOCK_DATA frames (128-512 KB payload), it copies the varint header (2-6 bytes) + full payload into a new `BytesMut`, then writes it all. This means a ~512 KB allocation + copy per chunk that could be avoided.

## How

### Option A: Separate header/payload writes
```rust
async fn send_frame(&mut self, type_id: u8, payload: &[u8]) -> Result<(), TransportError> {
    // Encode only the header (varint type_id + varint length) into a small buffer
    let mut header = BytesMut::with_capacity(20);
    encode_varint(type_id as u64, &mut header);
    encode_varint(payload.len() as u64, &mut header);
    
    self.send.write_all(&header).await?;
    self.send.write_all(payload).await?;
    Ok(())
}
```

This requires `encode_varint` to be public (currently it's private in `codec.rs`). The `encode_message` function would still exist for cases where a single buffer is needed.

### Option B: Reusable write buffer in QuicStream
```rust
pub struct QuicStream {
    send: quinn::SendStream,
    recv: quinn::RecvStream,
    read_buf: BytesMut,
    write_buf: BytesMut,  // reusable encode buffer
}
```
Clear `write_buf` at start of each `send_frame`, encode into it, write it.

### Recommended: Option A (cleaner, no state to manage)

## TDD Approach

1. Write test: sending a large frame (1 MB) via separate writes produces same wire format as single-buffer
2. Write test: decode_message can decode frames sent via Option A
3. Existing transport tests pass
4. Optional: benchmark comparing allocation count

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `send_frame` no longer allocates a `BytesMut` per frame for the payload portion
- [ ] Wire format is unchanged (existing codec decode tests pass)
- [ ] `encode_varint` made `pub` in `codec.rs` (or a new public `encode_header` function added)
- [ ] All existing tests pass

## Worktree

```bash
git worktree add ../rift-transport-opt phase6-transport
```
close_reason: send_frame uses stack-allocated header buffer + separate writes — no per-frame BytesMut

title: Cross-reference chunk hashes against Merkle tree leaves in read()
Implement chunk file garbage collection for cache
description: Orphaned chunk files accumulate on disk because no code calls ChunkStore::delete_chunk when manifests are removed or updated. Options: ref-counted GC on remove_manifest/put_manifest, periodic background GC scan, or LRU eviction with size limits. This is a follow-up after the no-cache branch is merged.
notes:
IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS:

Affected files:
- crates/rift-client/src/cache/db.rs — remove_manifest() and put_manifest() are the two places where chunk data becomes orphaned
- crates/rift-client/src/cache/chunks.rs — ChunkStore already has delete_chunk(hash) method that can be called

Current state of deletion paths:
1. remove_manifest() (db.rs ~line 251): Deletes the manifest and its chunk_refs via CASCADE, but does NOT call delete_chunk() for the actual data files in ChunkStore.
2. put_manifest() (db.rs ~line 190): Uses INSERT OR REPLACE to upsert chunk_refs. If a file shrinks (fewer chunks), stale refs for chunk_indices >= new count are pruned via DELETE WHERE chunk_index >= N. But the actual chunk data files for those pruned refs are never deleted from ChunkStore.

Approach options:
A. Reference-counted GC (best for content-addressable chunks): After remove_manifest or put_manifest, query chunk_hashes that are no longer referenced by ANY chunk_refs row, then call delete_chunk for each orphan.
   SQL: SELECT chunk_hash FROM chunk_refs WHERE chunk_hash NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT chunk_hash FROM chunk_refs)
   This is correct because chunks are content-addressable — the same hash can appear in multiple files' manifests, so we can only delete when no manifest references it.

B. Periodic background GC: Scan all manifest chunk_refs, build a set of referenced hashes, then walk the ChunkStore directory and delete any .bin file whose hash is not in the set. Can be triggered on startup or on a timer.

C. LRU eviction with size limits: Track access times, evict least-recently-accessed chunks when total size exceeds a configurable limit. Also needs to track which manifests reference which chunks.

Recommended approach: Start with (A) — synchronous GC after remove_manifest and after put_manifest's stale ref pruning. This is the simplest correct approach and prevents unbounded growth. Add (C) as a follow-up for configurable cache size limits (separate bd issue: rift-xay).

title: read_response loads entire file on cache hit
description: In handler/read.rs, read_response always calls tokio::fs::read to load the full file, even when chunk boundaries are already cached in the DB. For a 10GB file where the client requests chunks 5-10, the server still reads all 10GB into memory. Fix: on cache hit (boundaries in DB via get_children/get_leaf_info), open the file, seek to each chunk offset, and read_exact only the requested bytes. On cache miss, the whole-file read is unavoidable (Chunker needs &[u8]) but should serve chunks from the already-allocated buffer instead of re-reading.

title: Server multi-share config support
description: Server currently only serves first share from config. Need to wire all shares in config to accept_loop so multiple shares can be exported. Related: config parsing added but accept_loop hardcoded to shares[0].path.
notes:
[Code Review Context]

This is more than a missing feature — the current behavior is dangerously misleading. The config parser accepts multiple [[share]] entries silently, and the CLI allows multiple --share flags, but runtime only exports shares[0]. There is no warning, error, or log message for dropped shares.

Specific code paths to address:
1. main.rs line 63: `let share_path = server_config.shares[0].path.clone()` — only first share used
2. server.rs: RiftWelcome hardcodes a single ShareInfo — clients cannot discover other shares
3. server.rs: accept_loop takes a single PathBuf share parameter — needs Vec<ShareConfig> or similar
4. No validation in main.rs that warns about or rejects multi-share configs

A parallel bead (rift-lu1t) has been filed for the misleading config behavior — either fix that by adding warnings/rejection until multi-share is implemented, or implement multi-share (this bead).

Priority upgraded from P3 to P2: the silent config truncation is a user-facing correctness issue. Admin thinks only share X is exported, but shares[0] happens to be share Y — potential data exposure.

title: Stream file reads on the server instead of loading entire file into memory
description:
The server reads the entire file into memory before processing it in three handlers (crates/rift-server/src/handler.rs):
- get_or_compute_merkle_root (line ~430): tokio::fs::read(path) to compute Merkle hash
- read_response (line ~543): tokio::fs::read(path) to send chunks
- merkle_drill_response (line ~713): tokio::fs::read(path) to compute leaf hashes

For large files (multi-GB) this will OOM the server. read_response is the most critical: it should open the file and stream chunks directly to the wire without buffering the whole file.

What needs to be done:
1. read_response: replace tokio::fs::read with tokio::fs::File::open, then read+send each chunk independently using tokio::io::AsyncReadExt. FastCDC chunking (Chunker::chunk) currently operates on a complete &[u8] — you will need to evaluate whether to: (a) mmap the file so Chunker still works on a slice, or (b) replace Chunker with a streaming CDC variant, or (c) read the file once into memory just for chunking boundary computation, then stream actual chunk data from file by seeking.
2. get_or_compute_merkle_root (called from stat): same issue, lower urgency since stat is less frequent. Option (c) from above (read for CDC, then discard buffer after hashing) is a pragmatic interim if full streaming is deferred.
3. merkle_drill_response: same as #2.

Note: the FastCDC Chunker in rift-common::crypto::Chunker takes &[u8] — assess whether it can be changed to an iterator/streaming API first, as that's the enabling change for true streaming in all three handlers.

The Chunker is in crates/rift-common/src/crypto.rs.
close_reason: Deferred: streaming reads require Chunker API change (currently requires &[u8]). read_response already computes Merkle tree only once (previous double-computation was fixed). mmap-based approach is viable but requires unsafe code. Marking as deferred for now — current approach is correct for files up to several GB.

title: Wire up _cached_root_hash in ShareView::read to skip stat round-trip
description:
ShareView::read (crates/rift-client/src/view.rs:186) accepts cached_root_hash: Option<&[u8]> but immediately binds it as _cached_root_hash and discards it. The intent is to let the FUSE layer pass an already-known root hash (from the inode cache) so the mandatory stat_batch round-trip at line 195 can be skipped.

What needs to be done:
1. In view.rs::read, if cached_root_hash is Some(hash), skip the stat_batch call and use (file_size=u64::MAX as sentinel or require caller to supply it too) — or alternatively change the signature to accept cached attrs (size + root_hash together, since size is also needed to clamp the read range at line 226/249).
2. The cleanest API change: replace cached_root_hash: Option<&[u8]> with cached_attrs: Option<&FileAttrs>, so the caller can supply both size and root_hash. Skip stat_batch when Some.
3. Update the ShareView trait definition (crates/rift-client/src/view.rs:38) and all trait impls/callers, including the FUSE layer (crates/rift-client/src/fuse/ or platform-specific mount code).
4. Update MockRemote in view.rs tests to not set a stat_batch result when the cached path is taken.

The optimization matters for sequential reads of the same file: currently every read() call pays a full stat RPC round-trip even when the FUSE inode cache already has fresh attrs.

title: stat_response uses blocking std::fs calls inside join_all on async runtime
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handler.rs, stat_response was refactored to use futures::future::join_all for concurrency. However, resolve() calls std::fs::canonicalize and the body calls std::fs::metadata — both synchronous blocking operations. Running blocking calls inside join_all on tokio tasks blocks the async runtime thread pool, preventing other tasks from making progress.

Fix: Replace std::fs::metadata with tokio::fs::metadata. For resolve(), which calls std::fs::canonicalize, wrap the call in tokio::task::spawn_blocking so it runs on the blocking thread pool. Alternatively, revert to a sequential for-loop if concurrency is not needed (the previous for-loop was sequential and worked fine).
acceptance_criteria: No std::fs calls remain in the async stat_response hot path (except behind spawn_blocking); Tests continue to pass
close_reason: Replaced all blocking std::fs calls with tokio::fs equivalents; resolve() now async; no std::fs calls remain in handler hot path

title: HandleDatabase grows unboundedly; no eviction for deleted/renamed paths
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handle.rs, HandleDatabase is an in-memory BidirectionalMap that maps ULIDs ↔ relative paths. Entries are only added (via get_or_create_handle and populate_from_share); they are never removed. When a file is deleted or renamed by the share owner, the stale ULID still resolves to an old relative path — the subsequent canonicalize call will fail (error, but still wasteful), and the map entry persists indefinitely. Over time with many file operations this is a memory leak.

The old path-based approach had the same problem (dead paths accumulate), but with ULIDs there's no way for the client to "refresh" a handle — it's opaque.

Fix: Implement cleanup strategies, e.g.:
- On resolve failure (canonicalize returns ENOENT), proactively remove the stale ULID from the map.
- Add a periodic or lru-based eviction for entries not recently accessed.
- Document the current limitation and planned fix.

Start with the minimal fix: remove the entry when resolve() fails due to a missing path. This prevents stale entries from accumulating on repeated access attempts.
acceptance_criteria: When resolve fails because the path no longer exists, the stale ULID is removed from the BidirectionalMap; A test verifies that a deleted file's handle is cleaned up on failed resolve; get_or_create_handle correctly re-creates a handle if the old one was evicted
close_reason: Added HandleDatabase::remove(), resolve() evicts stale handles on ENOENT and symlink escape; tests verify eviction and re-creation

title: Refactor HandleDatabase to use canonicalized full paths instead of relative paths
description:
## Current State

HandleDatabase currently uses relative paths (stripping share root prefix) for ULID lookups. This has edge cases with:
- Empty paths (share root becomes  instead of .)
- Repeating path patterns (e.g., /tmp/xxx/tmp/xxx/)
- Path ambiguity issues

## Proposed Refactor

Switch to using canonicalized full paths as keys in HandleDatabase:

### Pros:
1. Simpler code - no strip_prefix logic, no edge cases
2. Direct lookup - canonical path is the key
3. xattr-stored ULIDs still provide file identity persistence
4. Security maintained by forbidding absolute symlinks + canonicalize + starts_with check

### Implementation Steps:

1. **Update HandleDatabase methods**:
   -  - use canonical(path) as key instead of relative path
   -  - returns canonical path directly
   -  - populate with canonical paths

2. **Add symlink restriction**:
   - At : skip or error on absolute symlinks
   - At : check if path is absolute symlink, reject if so

3. **Update resolve()**:
   - Look up canonical path from ULID
   - Canonicalize again (catches changes)
   - Verify starts_with(share_canonical) - security check

4. **Update all tests**:
   - Handler unit tests
   - Integration tests
   - New symlink security tests

### Acceptance Criteria:
- All existing tests pass
- New test: absolute symlink is rejected
- Simpler path handling code (no strip_prefix edge cases)
- Performance equivalent or better

### Notes:
- This is NOT about changing the wire protocol (handles are still 16-byte ULIDs)
- This is internal storage representation change
- xattr storage still provides persistence across restarts
- Security invariant: starts_with check must remain in resolve()
close_reason: HandleDatabase now stores canonical paths; share_root param removed; resolve() simplified; strip_prefix edge cases eliminated

title: Migrate rift-server Database to tokio-rusqlite
description:
Refactor Database struct to use tokio-rusqlite Connection instead of rusqlite with std::sync::Mutex.

Changes needed:
1. Database::open() becomes async
2. Database::open_in_memory() becomes async
3. Database::get_merkle() becomes async, uses conn.call()
4. Database::put_merkle() becomes async, uses conn.call()
5. Remove std::sync::Mutex entirely

This eliminates the blocking issue since tokio-rusqlite runs SQLite in a background thread.

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Database struct uses tokio_rusqlite::Connection directly (no Mutex)
- [ ] All methods are async
- [ ] handler.rs updated to await database calls
- [ ] server.rs updated to pass async Database
- [ ] Tests pass

Also remove the "bundled" feature from rusqlite as it's no longer needed directly.

title: Replace std::sync::Mutex with tokio::sync::Mutex in server and transport
description:
The codebase uses both std::sync::Mutex and tokio::sync::Mutex. The std::sync::Mutex is used in async contexts (server database and transport policy), which can block the tokio runtime thread.

Locations:
- rift-server/src/metadata/db.rs: Database struct uses std::sync::Mutex<Connection>
- rift-transport/src/policy.rs: TofuPolicy uses std::sync::Mutex<TofuStore>

This is problematic because blocking on std::sync::Mutex in async code can starve other tasks on the same worker thread.

Fix: Replace with tokio::sync::Mutex, using .lock().await instead of .lock().unwrap()

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Replace std::sync::Mutex in db.rs with tokio::sync::Mutex
- [ ] Replace std::sync::Mutex in policy.rs with tokio::sync::Mutex
- [ ] Update Database::connection() to return async guard
- [ ] Run tests to verify
notes:
The issue with updating Database::connection():

1. Current: Returns std::sync::MutexGuard directly
2. With tokio::sync::Mutex: Returns tokio::sync::MutexGuard
3. tokio::sync::MutexGuard is NOT Sync - this affects Database's Send+Sync bounds
4. The server passes Database as Option<&Database> to sync handler functions

Workaround: Either wrap in Arc<tokio::sync::Mutex<Connection>> or keep synchronous usage pattern but switch to tokio::sync::Mutex anyway (still better than std::sync in async context).

title: Add pre-commit git hooks for Rust checks

Set up pre-commit framework to run format, lint, and type checks on staged files before commit.

title: Replace mtime_ns u64 sentinel 0 with Option<u64>
description:
All call sites use .unwrap_or(0) for unreadable modification times. A file with genuine epoch timestamp (mtime_ns == 0) is indistinguishable from a failed metadata() call. This can cause false CacheStatus::Stale matches: if a file at epoch has the same size as a previously cached entry, the cache key (0, size) collides.

Replace u64 mtime_ns fields with Option<u64> (or a newtype Mtime(u64) with Mtime::UNKNOWN sentinel) across:
- FileInfo in background_check.rs
- cache_status, put_merkle, put_tree signatures
- cache_computed_tree and handler read/drill call sites
- All .unwrap_or(0) call sites (5 files)

This is a wide-reaching refactoring that should be a separate PR after the bg-check merge.

## Context
Code review finding #14 from bg-check PR. Low urgency but represents a real (if rare) correctness edge case.
acceptance_criteria: No u64 .unwrap_or(0) for mtime_ns remains. All mtime_ns fields are Option<u64> or Mtime newtype. Tests distinguish between epoch and unknown mtime.

title: Add content hash verification to write_chunk fast path
description:
LOW PRIORITY: ChunkStore::write_chunk has a fast path that checks file size but not content. If a file with the right hash-derived path exists with matching size but corrupted content, write_chunk skips writing and leaves stale data.

The current logic (chunks.rs ~line 42-50):
  if metadata.len() == data.len() { return Ok(()); }  // skip rewrite

This is safe under normal conditions (BLAKE3 makes collisions infeasible), but could mask filesystem corruption. Could explain the 'consistently 1 chunk fails' pattern.

Fix: Add optional content verification or document the size-only assumption clearly.
close_reason: Not reproducible. The cache corruption warnings were caused by missing chunks being conflated with corrupted chunks (fixed in rift-er4w). The write_chunk size-only fast path is theoretically imperfect but BLAKE3 makes hash collisions infeasible.

title: drill_response and merkle_cache whole-file read on cache miss
description: handler/drill.rs and merkle_cache.rs both call tokio::fs::read on cache miss to feed Chunker which requires &[u8]. This is currently unavoidable — FastCDC needs contiguous bytes to compute chunk boundaries. Two improvement options: (1) use mmap for large files to avoid allocating a full buffer (requires unsafe, platform-specific), (2) add a streaming/streamable Chunker API that reads from a file handle in a single pass. Both options are non-trivial architectural changes. Marking P3 — only matters for files > available RAM. Related to rift-50p.

title: resolve re-canonicalizes share root on every request
description: In crates/rift-server/src/handler/mod.rs, resolve() calls tokio::fs::canonicalize(share).await on every single request. The share root does not change between requests. Cache the canonical share root in server state and pass it to resolve() instead of re-computing it.
notes: Part of code-review remediation plan. See commit c5c114f for items 2+3. Will be fixed via subagent-driven plan at docs/superpowers/plans/2026-04-24-fix-code-review-findings.md.

title: Structural cleanup: move handler.rs inline tests to tests/server.rs
description: Code quality review of rift-772.4.2–4.9 found that 12 of 15 new inline tests in handler.rs call pub functions already covered by tests/server.rs. The 3 genuinely new tests (build_attrs_includes_root_hash, build_attrs_empty_file_has_zero_size, readdir_response_empty_directory) and improved error-variant assertions should be moved to tests/server.rs, replacing the weaker counterparts there. The duplicate inline tests should be deleted. Note: the 4 renamed malformed-payload tests are fine as-is.
close_reason: Moot: handler.rs was split into handler/ directory (rift-f8x). Only 7 inline tests remain across attrs.rs (3) and handle.rs (4), with minimal overlap to tests/server.rs. No structural cleanup needed.

title: Add tests for ConnectionStats trait implementation
description: ConnectionStats trait in crates/rift-client/src/client.rs (line 497) and its impl for RiftClient<RecordingConnection<InMemoryConnection>> (stream_count, recorded_frames at lines 516/521) have zero tests. Add tests: (1) stream_count starts at 0, (2) stream_count increments with each operation, (3) recorded_frames captures sent frames, (4) recorded_frames returns correct type_ids and payloads. Use RiftClient::from_connection with RecordingConnection::new(InMemoryConnection).

title: Add tests for QuicConnection::close() and QuicListener accessor methods

Add tests: (1) close() causes subsequent open_stream to fail, (2) close() is idempotent (calling twice doesn't panic), (3) close() on one side is detected by the other side. These can be tested using the existing QUIC test infrastructure in tests/quic_transport.rs (gen_test_cert helper, server_endpoint/client_endpoint setup).

title: Add roundtrip test for LINK_REQUEST/RESPONSE messages
description: LINK_REQUEST (0x1E) and LINK_RESPONSE (0x1F) in crates/rift-protocol/src/messages.rs are marked as 'deferred' but still have defined constants. Add roundtrip tests even if the message structs are placeholder. If no protobuf message struct exists yet, create a minimal test that verifies the constant values are unique and document that implementation is deferred.

title: Add isolated tests for SharePermission struct
description: SharePermission in crates/rift-common/src/config.rs (line 38) is only tested as part of config deserialization, never in isolation. Add tests: (1) SharePermission with AccessLevel::ReadOnly, (2) SharePermission with AccessLevel::ReadWrite, (3) Default AccessLevel is ReadWrite (already tested but should also be tested on SharePermission directly), (4) Debug/Clone derive works.

title: Add tests for BidirectionalMap::with_capacity() and is_empty()
description: BidirectionalMap in crates/rift-common/src/handle_map.rs has with_capacity() (line 27) and is_empty() (line 87) that are untested. Add tests: (1) with_capacity(N) creates empty map, (2) with_capacity(0) works, (3) is_empty() returns true on new map, (4) is_empty() returns false after insert, (5) is_empty() returns true after removing all entries. These are simple but must be verified.

title: Add tests for MerkleTree with edge-case fanout values
description: MerkleTree::new() in crates/rift-common/src/crypto.rs (line 97) takes a fanout parameter. No tests for fanout=0 or fanout=1. Add tests: (1) fanout=0 — should this panic or produce a valid tree? (2) fanout=1 — degenerate linear chain, verify behavior, (3) fanout=2 — binary tree (already tested implicitly), (4) very large fanout (1000+) with few leaves. Default fanout is 256.

title: Add tests for Blake3Hash::from_array() constructor
description: Blake3Hash::from_array() in crates/rift-common/src/crypto.rs (line 16) is a const fn that creates a Blake3Hash from a [u8; 32] array. It has no test. Add tests: (1) verify from_array produces same hash as computing Blake3Hash::new on known input, (2) verify as_bytes() returns the original array, (3) verify roundtrip from_array(arr).as_bytes() == &arr.

title: HandleDatabase silently ignores xattr write failures
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/handle.rs line 100, xattr::set failures are discarded with . This means:
- On filesystems that support xattrs but hit a permissions error, the failure is invisible — handles won't persist across restarts, and the operator has no way to diagnose why.
- On filesystems that don't support xattrs (e.g. tmpfs in tests), the best-effort silence is correct, but there's no distinction between "expected failure" and "unexpected failure."

Fix:
- Log a warning when xattr::set fails with an error other than ENOTSUP/EOPNOTSUPP (i.e. distinguish "expected" vs "unexpected" failures).
- For xattr::get (line 88), similarly log a warning on unexpected I/O errors (currently Ok(None) and Err both fall into the same "generate new ULID" branch).
acceptance_criteria: xattr::set failures that are NOT due to unsupported filesystem produce a tracing::warn log message; xattr::get errors that are NOT ENOTSUP also produce a warning; Handles still work correctly on filesystems without xattr support

title: Remove ad-hoc timeout on STAT_RESPONSE send; apply consistently or not at all
description:
In crates/rift-server/src/server.rs handle_stream, the msg::STAT_REQUEST branch has a 5-second tokio::time::timeout wrapping send_frame + finish_send, while all other message branches (LOOKUP, READDIR, READ, MERKLE_DRILL) send without any timeout. This inconsistency has no documented justification.

If the concern is disconnected clients, the timeout should be applied consistently to all send paths (or better, at the connection level). If there is no evidence that send_frame can hang, the timeout adds complexity and a failure mode (dropping valid responses that take >5s on slow connections).

Fix: Remove the STAT-specific timeout. If client-disconnect resilience is needed, implement it as a connection-level concern (e.g. a write deadline on the QUIC stream) rather than per-message timeouts scattered through the handler. The other branches already demonstrate the simpler pattern of just propagating the error via .
acceptance_criteria: All message branches use the same send pattern (no special-casing STAT); No per-message timeouts in handle_stream
close_reason: Removed ad-hoc STAT timeout; all message branches now use consistent send_frame().await? + finish_send().await? pattern

title: Migrate from custom ULID to UUID v7 for file handles
description: Migrate the current custom ULID implementation in crates/rift-server/src/handle.rs to the standardized UUID v7 format (RFC 9562). UUID v7 provides the same time-ordered, 128-bit properties as ULID but with IETF standardization, better ecosystem support (uuid crate with 200M+ downloads), and improved tooling/debugging capabilities. The wire format remains identical (16 bytes binary), so this change is completely transparent to the client protocol.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Replace custom Ulid struct with uuid::Uuid using v7 feature
   2. Update HandleDatabase to use Uuid type instead of [u8; 16]
   3. Maintain 16-byte binary xattr storage (fully compatible)
   4. Update all handler code to use Uuid type
   5. Switch string representations to standard hyphenated UUID format
   6. All existing tests pass without modification
   7. Add uuid crate dependency with v7 feature to workspace

title: Implement parallel chunking with multi-threaded FastCDC
description:
Add parallel chunking for files > threshold (e.g., 10MB). Use rayon for parallel FastCDC computation.

**Dependencies** (blockers): rift-jdi (FastCDC core)

Location: rift-common/crypto.rs Chunker. This is a subtask of rift-jdi.
close_reason: Wontfix: FastCDC is inherently sequential (each boundary depends on preceding bytes). Chunk-then-hash parallelism is already handled by BLAKE3's internal rayon. Real bottleneck is file I/O (rift-50p), not CPU parallelism.

title: Implement streaming directory pagination
description: Fix readdir to use streaming/pagination instead of collecting entire directory into memory. Current: handler.rs:285 loads all entries before returning. Should yield entries incrementally to support large directories (100k+ entries). Implementation: use std::fs::read_dir iterator directly with skip/take instead of collect().

title: Implement reconnection cache sync
description:
Implement reconnection cache sync. On reconnect, compare client's cached Merkle tree with server's to identify what changed during disconnect.

**Blocked by**: rift-40i (resumable transfers), rift-v49 (Merkle tree cache persistence)

**Dependencies**: Needs both resumable transfers AND cached Merkle trees.

title: Implement cache leases (RIFT_LEASES)
description: Strong cache coherency guarantee beyond optimistic caching. Server grants read lease alongside file access - client knows cached version is current within lease window. Lease revocation before write commit ensures no staleness. Default 60s lease duration. See docs/01-requirements/features/leases.md.

title: Implement write-side file deduplication (RIFT_FILE_DEDUP)
description: When client writes a file whose content already exists elsewhere on server (identical copy under different name), skip full transfer. Client includes new file's Merkle root in WRITE_REQUEST. Server checks if any file already has that root, if so copies locally instead of receiving data. See docs/01-requirements/features/write-dedup.md.

title: Implement configurable Merkle tree fanout
description: Make Merkle tree fanout configurable per-share (advertised in RiftWelcome). Current default is 64. Higher fanout (256, 512) reduces tree depth for large files (important for WAN), lower fanout reduces per-level message size. No capability flag needed - server-advertised parameter. See docs/01-requirements/features/merkle-fanout-config.md.

title: Implement bandwidth throttling and scheduling
description: Implement rate limiting for transfers over WAN. Client-side: --bandwidth-limit, --upload-limit, --download-limit. Server-side: per-share or per-client limits in config. Time-based scheduling: throttle during business hours, unlimited at night. No protocol changes needed - implemented at QUIC transport layer. See docs/01-requirements/features/bandwidth-throttling.md.

title: Implement offline mode with conflict detection
description: Allow Rift mount to stay available when network disconnects. Files in cached/pinned state remain readable. Writes are journaled locally and synced on reconnect. Conflicts (both client and server modified) are resolved by saving client version as .rift-conflict file. Requires selective sync and Merkle tree. See docs/01-requirements/features/offline-mode.md.

title: Implement native Linux kernel module (replace FUSE)
description: Replace FUSE client with native kernel filesystem module for better performance. Architecture: hybrid kernel/userspace (rift.ko handles VFS, userspace daemon handles QUIC/TLS). Biggest gains: metadata operations (2-10x), small random I/O (2-5x), mmap (5-20x). See docs/01-requirements/features/kernel-module.md for detailed architecture and performance analysis.

title: Implement readdir glob filter (RIFT_READDIR_FILTER)
description: Implement server-side glob filtering for readdir. Client sends a glob pattern, server filters entries before returning. Reduces network transfer for large directories with filtering. See docs/01-requirements/features/readdir-filter.md.

title: Implement case-insensitive filenames (RIFT_CASE_INSENSITIVE)
description: Implement case-insensitive filename support for filesystems that need it (e.g., macOS-compatible shares). Requires handling case collisions (two files with same name, different case). See docs/01-requirements/features/case-insensitive.md.

title: Implement supplementary group mapping (RIFT_SUPGROUPS)
description: Implement supplementary group mapping for client UIDs. Currently server-side identity inherits whatever supplementary groups that user already has on the server. This feature allows explicit mapping of client supplementary groups to server groups. See docs/01-requirements/features/supplementary-groups.md.

title: Implement ACL support (RIFT_ACLS)
description: Implement Access Control Lists for fine-grained permissions beyond POSIX mode bits. Important for enterprise adoption when multiple clients with different users access the same share. Requires deciding between POSIX ACLs vs NFSv4 ACLs. See docs/01-requirements/features/acls.md for details.

title: Create rift CLI crate
description:
Create a new 'rift' crate (rift-cli) that serves as the single entry point for all rift CLI commands. Move functionality from rift-client and rift-server binaries into this crate.

Commands to support:
- rift mount - mount a remote share (currently in rift-client)
- rift server - server management commands:
  - rift server start - start the server
  - rift server regenerate-certificate - regenerate the server certificate

This consolidates CLI handling in one place and provides a unified command structure.

title: Migrate TofuPolicy to tokio::sync::Mutex
description:
The TofuPolicy in rift-transport/src/policy.rs uses std::sync::Mutex which can block in async context.

Replace with tokio::sync::Mutex to fix the blocking issue.

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Replace std::sync::Mutex with tokio::sync::Mutex in policy.rs
- [ ] Update lock() calls to use .lock().await
- [ ] Tests pass

title: Migrate rift-client FileCache to tokio-rusqlite
description:
Refactor FileCache in rift-client/src/cache/db.rs to use tokio-rusqlite instead of rusqlite with std::sync::Mutex.

This is related to rift-41n (decide what to do with FileCache) - after that decision is made, implement with tokio-rusqlite.

Changes needed (if keeping FileCache):
1. FileCache uses tokio_rusqlite::Connection directly
2. All methods become async
3. Remove std::sync::Mutex entirely

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] If FileCache is kept: migrate to tokio-rusqlite
- [ ] Tests pass
close_reason: tokio-rusqlite migration done. Integration remains in rift-50j

title: Integrate FileCache into client
description:
FileCache in rift-client/src/cache/db.rs is unused. Integrate it into the client for caching manifests and chunks.

The cache should store:
- File manifests: (handle -> root_hash, chunk list)
- Chunk data: (chunk_hash -> data) content-addressable storage

This enables the client to cache data from the server and avoid re-fetching.

After integration, migrate to tokio-rusqlite (see rift-e79).

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Integrate FileCache into client (RiftClient or ShareView)
- [ ] Write tests for cache behavior
- [ ] Pass to rift-e79 for tokio-rusqlite migration

title: Investigate and decide what to do with unused FileCache in rift-client
description:
The FileCache struct in rift-client/src/cache/db.rs appears to be unused in the codebase - no imports or references found. It wraps a std::sync::Mutex<Connection>.

Current state:
-rift-client/src/lib.rs exports the cache module
- rift-client/src/cache/db.rs defines FileCache with std::sync::Mutex
- No code actually uses this cache

Options to decide:
1. Remove as dead code - delete the module entirely
2. Integrate into client - actually use it for caching manifests/chunks
3. Keep but fix the Mutex type - leave it unused but fix the std::sync::Mutex issue

We should decide which path to take before fixing the Mutex issue.

Acceptance criteria:
- [ ] Decide on path forward (remove/integrate/keep)
- [ ] If integrating: specify where/how it should be used
- [ ] If removing: create cleanup task
close_reason: Decision made: integrate FileCache into client

title: Implement pluggable storage backends
description: Abstract server storage layer behind a trait. Enables S3/object storage, database-backed metadata, in-memory backends for testing, future clustered/erasure-coded storage. Server defines StorageBackend trait with operations matching protocol needs (stat, lookup, readdir, create, read, write, etc.). See docs/01-requirements/features/pluggable-backends.md.

title: Implement multi-server striping for increased throughput
description: Stripe data across multiple servers for increased throughput and capacity, similar to pNFS (parallel NFS). Metadata server directs clients to data servers. Dramatically increases complexity - far future post-v1 feature. See docs/01-requirements/features/multi-server-striping.md.

title: Implement partial file updates (sub-file writes)
description: Allow updating specific byte ranges without rewriting entire file. Instead of contiguous bytes, server stores ordered list of chunk references. Changing chunks 50-52 of 1000-chunk file: client sends 3 new chunks, server stores in content-addressed store, updates reference list. Eliminates full file read/write for small edits. See docs/01-requirements/features/partial-writes.md.

title: Implement file versioning (time travel)
description: Built-in version history for files. On each write commit, server retains previous Merkle root, timestamp, sequence number. Users can list versions, restore previous versions, browse old directory states. Retention policy configurable per-share (days, versions count). Uses existing content-addressed chunk storage. See docs/01-requirements/features/file-versioning.md.

title: Implement share-level access tokens
description: Generate time-limited tokens for ad-hoc share access. Token includes permissions (ro/rw), expiry, optional subdirectory scope. Recipients use token in URL (rift://server/share?token=...) without needing certificates. Signed structure validated by server. See docs/01-requirements/features/access-tokens.md.

title: Implement cross-share deduplication
description: Content-addressed chunk storage - identical chunks across shares/files stored once. Uses existing BLAKE3 hashes. Server maintains chunk store keyed by hash with reference counting. Client cache also content-addressed - if chunk already cached from any share, don't re-fetch. Opt-in and admin-controlled. See docs/01-requirements/features/cross-share-dedup.md.
